topics.title: The One Date On Your Insurance Policy That Can Quietly Erase Five Years of Coverage
topics.subtitle: A plain-English look at the claims-made retroactive date: the most expensive line of fine print most school leaders were never told to check.

hub.content_markdown:
# The One Date On Your Insurance Policy That Can Quietly Erase Five Years of Coverage

*A plain-English look at the claims-made retroactive date: the most expensive line of fine print most school leaders were never told to check.*

---

A claim lands in 2026 for something that happened in 2021.

You were insured the whole time. You renewed every year, paid every premium, never lapsed, never got a warning.

You're still not covered.

Here's the date that did it.

---

## The thing nobody walks you through

You run a school. You're balancing a board, a budget, a building, two hundred families, and a staff who need their benefits to actually work. When the insurance renewal shows up once a year, you do the rational thing. You skim the premium, check that it didn't jump too hard, and sign.

That's the exact behavior this whole problem is built on.

The number that matters most on some of your most important policies isn't the premium. It's a single date sitting in the declarations page, called the retroactive date. The moment it moves the wrong way, you can lose coverage for years you already paid for, and you won't find out until a claim shows up.

So let me walk you through it. What it means, then what it looks like when it goes wrong, then how to stop it.

## What "claims-made" actually means

Some of your insurance is occurrence coverage. Occurrence is simple and forgiving. If the bad thing happened while the policy was in force, you're covered, even if the claim doesn't get filed until years later. The year the event happened is the year that pays, and once you've bought it, that year is locked in for good.

A lot of the policies that protect you where you're most exposed don't work that way. They're claims-made. That usually includes:

- Directors & Officers (D&O), which protects your board and leadership
- Employment Practices Liability (EPLI), for wrongful termination, discrimination, and harassment claims from staff
- Educators' professional liability
- Abuse and molestation coverage, when it's written claims-made

Claims-made runs on a different logic. It covers a claim based on the year the claim gets reported, not the year the event happened. The claim has to be filed while the policy is active.

So far that sounds reasonable. The part that rarely gets explained is this: a claims-made policy only covers events that happened on or after one specific date. Anything before that date is excluded, no matter how many years you've been a paying customer.

That date is the retroactive date.

## The retroactive date, plainly

Your retroactive date is the earliest date a covered event can have happened and still get paid. Move that date forward, and everything before it drops out of your coverage.

When you first buy claims-made coverage, the retro date usually gets set to that day. Fair enough. But if you keep claims-made coverage running year after year, the entire point is to keep that original retro date frozen where it is. Every year you renew, your covered window stretches further back. Five straight years of coverage should mean five years of protected history behind you.

It should, anyway.

## How it goes wrong

The renewal cycle comes around, and a generalist broker, the kind who treats charter schools as too small to fuss over, goes shopping to knock a few thousand dollars off your premium. They find a cheaper carrier. They move your policy. They tell you they saved you money.

What they often skip, because checking it is tedious and they're in a hurry, is what the new carrier set as your retroactive date.

When a new claims-made carrier writes your policy, the default is to set the retro date to the day the new policy starts. Not your original date. The new one.

In a single renewal, your protected history can collapse from five years down to nothing. Every act before the switch, every decision your board made, every personnel call, every incident that hasn't surfaced yet, now sits outside the policy. You're paying about the same premium for a fraction of the protection, and the paperwork looks completely normal on its face.

The premium dropped four thousand dollars. The coverage you actually bought dropped by years. And nobody mentioned it to the board.

## Why this hits schools harder than anyone

For most businesses, an advanced retro date is a rough day. For a school, it can take the whole institution down.

It comes down to the kind of claims you face. The most catastrophic exposures a school carries, like abuse and molestation, civil rights, and certain employment matters, are also the ones with the longest gap between when the event happened and when the claim gets reported. Abuse claims especially tend to surface years, sometimes decades, after the fact, because of how and when victims come forward.

Now stack those two facts on top of each other:

1. Your worst claims are the ones most likely to be reported years late.
2. A claims-made policy with an advanced retro date specifically refuses to cover events from the years before the switch.

So the retroactive date trap doesn't put your small claims at risk. It puts the exact category of claim that bankrupts schools and pulls board members' personal assets into a courtroom. It lands on your single biggest vulnerability, and it gets set off by an ordinary money-saving carrier switch that reads like good news on the invoice.

## The fix isn't complicated. It just has to get done.

This is a solvable problem. It gets ignored because solving it means somebody has to actually read the policy, line by line, every renewal. Here's what protecting your retro date takes:

Keep the original retroactive date. When you switch carriers, the new policy's retro date should match your original date, not the new start date. You ask for that, and you get it confirmed in writing. It does not happen on its own.

Get prior acts coverage, sometimes called "nose." A new carrier can agree to cover events going back to your old retro date. That's the bridge that keeps your history intact through a switch.

Or buy tail coverage from the carrier you're leaving. An Extended Reporting Period, the "tail," lets you keep reporting old-event claims to the old carrier after that policy ends. A preserved retro date, nose, or tail: you need at least one of them working every time a claims-made policy changes hands.

Read the declarations page at every renewal. The retro date lives there. It takes two minutes to find, and it's the highest-stakes line on the page.

That's the whole fix. The reason it doesn't happen isn't that it's hard. It's that the broker on your account decided your school wasn't worth the two minutes.

## What it looks like when someone's actually doing the job

A broker who takes this seriously does three things you've probably never gotten from a generalist.

They renew early, with weeks of runway, so there's actual time to look at the structure of a carrier switch instead of rubber-stamping it the night before the policy expires.

They pull the claims-made policies and check the retro date at every renewal, and they show you, in plain language, what it is and whether it moved.

They treat your board's personal protection like it matters, because the people sitting on a charter school board are putting their own assets on the line to serve a community, and an advanced retro date can leave them hanging personally.

None of that is exotic. It's the difference between a broker who treats your school like a real client and one who treats it like a file to clear off the desk.

## Before you renew anything

You don't have to take anyone's word for this, including this page. You just have to look at one thing.

So here's the question worth sitting with:

When was the last time anyone actually pulled your claims-made policies and confirmed, in writing, whether the retroactive date moved at your last carrier switch? Or did the renewal just process itself while everyone was busy running a school?

If you can't answer that with confidence, you don't have a coverage problem yet. You have a question nobody's checked. The check takes two minutes. What it costs to skip it doesn't show up until the worst possible day.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want a second set of eyes on your claims-made policies before your next renewal, that's the kind of thing we do before anyone asks.*

newsletter.label: LinkedIn personal newsletter
newsletter.content_markdown:
# LinkedIn Personal Newsletter: Retroactive Date Trap

*Spoke type: Personal profile newsletter (Aaron's voice). Goal: human story + compressed insight, driving to the full white paper. Post from Aaron's personal LinkedIn. ~750 words.*

---

**Headline:** A single date on her policy quietly erased five years of coverage. Nobody told her until the claim came in.

---

A school leader told me last year that her renewal "looked fine." The premium had even come down a little. Her generalist broker had shopped it, moved carriers, saved her a few thousand dollars, and sent over the paperwork. She signed it the way all of us sign things during a week that's already on fire.

What nobody told her was that the switch had reset one date on her liability policy. Her retroactive date. And resetting it wiped out coverage for years she thought she'd already paid for.

She found out the way no one should. A claim came in for something from years earlier, the kind of claim that takes a long time to surface. She'd been insured the whole time. She still wasn't covered. One line of fine print did that, and it got moved without anyone walking her through what it meant.

I grew up around manual labor and a ranch, where if you did a job halfway, somebody got hurt or something broke, and you owned it. That's the standard I expected when I got into insurance. It is not the standard a lot of charter schools are getting, and this is the clearest example I know.

Here's the mechanic, in plain English.

A lot of the coverage that protects your school where you're most exposed, your board, your leadership, certain employment and abuse exposures, is written "claims-made." A claims-made policy only pays for events that happened on or after one specific date: the retroactive date. Keep that date frozen in place year after year and your protected history keeps stretching back. That's the whole point of staying continuously covered.

When a broker switches you to a new carrier to shave premium, the new carrier's default is to set your retro date to the day the new policy starts. Not your original date. The new one. In one renewal, five years of protected history can collapse to zero, and the paperwork looks completely normal.

For a school, this is worse than for almost anyone. The claims that can end an institution, abuse and molestation in particular, are also the ones most likely to be reported years after the fact. So the trap is aimed squarely at the exposure that bankrupts schools and drags board members' personal assets into a courtroom. It gets triggered by a routine money-saving switch that reads like good news on the invoice.

The fix isn't complicated. It's just work nobody bothered to do:

- Preserve the original retro date when you switch carriers, confirmed in writing.
- Or carry "nose" coverage, where the new carrier covers events back to your old date.
- Or buy "tail" coverage from the carrier you're leaving.
- And read the declarations page every renewal. The retro date lives there. It takes two minutes.

The reason this gets missed isn't that it's hard. It's that the broker decided the school wasn't worth the two minutes. That's the part I can't stomach, and it's most of why Charter Select only works with charter schools and reads the whole policy every single renewal.

I wrote the full breakdown up on our site, no jargon, no gate, free to read and free to forward to your board or your business manager: **[LINK TO WHITE PAPER]**

If you only do one thing with this, do this: ask whoever handles your insurance one question.

When was the last time anyone actually pulled your claims-made policies and confirmed, in writing, whether the retroactive date moved at your last carrier switch? Or did the renewal just process itself while everyone was busy running a school?

If they can't answer that clearly, you just learned something about both your coverage and your broker.

Forward this to the school leader who needs it. This one's too expensive to find out the hard way.

Aaron Schwen

---

*Posting notes: replace [LINK TO WHITE PAPER] with the live hub URL. Keep the sign-off personal. First comment should drop the link again (LinkedIn suppresses posts with outbound links in the body, so some people put the link in the first comment instead, test both).*

checklist.content_markdown:
# Company Page Spoke: Claims-Made Carrier Switch Coverage Protection Checklist

*Spoke type: Company storefront page (authoritative, technical, credibility-building). Goal: validate Charter Select to a prospect actively vetting us. Post from the Charter Select company page. Lead with the matrix, then the checklist.*

---

**Headline:** Switching carriers on a claims-made policy? Confirm these before you sign, or you may be paying full premium for a fraction of your coverage history.

When a charter school moves carriers on claims-made coverage (D&O, EPLI, educators' professional, abuse and molestation where applicable), one detail decides whether years of prior coverage survive the switch: the retroactive date. Advance it, and every event before the new policy's start date falls outside coverage. Here is how the three protection mechanisms compare, and the checklist we run on every claims-made renewal.

## The three ways to protect your coverage history

| Mechanism | What it does | Who provides it | When you use it |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Preserved retroactive date** | New policy keeps your *original* retro date, so prior years stay covered | Incoming carrier | Standard ask on any carrier switch. Confirm in writing. |
| **Nose / Prior Acts coverage** | Incoming carrier agrees to cover events going back to your old retro date | Incoming carrier | When the new carrier won't simply match the original date |
| **Tail / Extended Reporting Period (ERP)** | Outgoing carrier lets you keep reporting prior-event claims after the policy ends | Outgoing carrier | When the incoming carrier won't cover prior acts at all |

You need at least one of these working every time a claims-made policy changes hands. Zero of them is the gap that erases your history.

## The renewal checklist

- [ ] Identify every claims-made policy in the program (don't assume; verify the form basis on each).
- [ ] Pull the current retroactive date on each claims-made policy, in writing.
- [ ] On any carrier switch, confirm the incoming retro date matches your original date, not the new inception date.
- [ ] If it can't match, confirm prior acts ("nose") coverage back to the original date.
- [ ] If nose isn't available, price and bind tail coverage (ERP) from the outgoing carrier before the old policy lapses.
- [ ] Read the declarations page and confirm the retro date in writing before binding.
- [ ] Document the confirmed retro date for the board record.

## Why this is standard practice for us and rare elsewhere

A generalist broker chasing a lower premium often sets the new policy up on the carrier's default terms, which advances the retro date silently. We renew early, read the full policy on every renewal, and confirm the retro date in writing every time, because the cost of getting this wrong lands on the exact exposure a school can least afford to lose.

**Read the full breakdown:** [LINK TO WHITE PAPER]

---

topics.title: The Claim No School Leader Wants to Picture Is Often Protected by the Smallest Number on the Policy
topics.subtitle: A plain-English look at sexual abuse and molestation coverage, why it is so often buried as a tiny sublimit inside general liability, and what real protection for this exposure looks like.

hub.content_markdown:
# The Claim No School Leader Wants to Picture Is Often Protected by the Smallest Number on the Policy

*A plain-English look at sexual abuse and molestation coverage, why it is so often buried as a tiny sublimit inside general liability, and what real protection for this exposure looks like.*

---

There is one category of claim that can end a charter school. Not strain it. End it.

It is also the claim most leaders never want to say out loud, so it rarely gets discussed at renewal. And because nobody brings it up, the coverage for it tends to be the thinnest, most overlooked figure on the entire policy.

Let me walk through this carefully, because the subject deserves care, and the mechanics deserve to be correct.

---

## Why this exposure exists at all

Any organization that brings adults and children together carries some risk of abuse. That is simply the nature of the work. Schools supervise children for hours a day, across classrooms, hallways, bathrooms, locker rooms, buses, field trips, and after-hours programs. The exposure is not a sign that something is wrong with a school. It is a structural reality of caring for kids.

Treating that reality seriously is not pessimism. It is the responsible posture, the same way a hospital plans for infection or a bank plans for fraud. The goal is twofold: do everything reasonable to prevent harm, and make sure that if an allegation ever surfaces, the school can respond, defend itself fairly, and stand behind any survivor without the financial side of it threatening the institution's survival.

Both halves of that goal touch insurance. And that is where most schools have a quiet problem they have never been shown.

## Why these claims are among the most severe a school can face

Three things make abuse claims uniquely dangerous to a school's finances.

First, the dollar figures. Settlements and verdicts in abuse cases can run very high, often well beyond what comparable claims in other categories reach, because of the lasting harm involved and the way juries weigh it.

Second, the timeline. Abuse allegations frequently surface years, sometimes decades, after the events in question, because of how and when survivors are able to come forward. Many states have widened or reopened the windows for these claims to be filed. So the exposure is what insurers call long-tail. The claim that arrives next year may concern a period long past.

Third, the cost of simply defending. Even an allegation that is ultimately found to have no merit must be investigated and defended, and that legal work is expensive on its own. A school does not get to choose whether to respond. It has to.

Put those together and you have a claim type that is severe, slow to surface, costly to defend, and capable of pulling in multiple claimants at once. This is exactly the kind of exposure insurance is supposed to be built around.

## How the coverage is usually structured, in plain English

Coverage for this risk is generally referred to as Sexual Abuse and Molestation coverage, shortened to SAM, or sometimes SAML or sexual misconduct liability. It tends to show up in one of three ways.

**As a small sublimit inside the general liability policy.** This is the most common, and the most quietly dangerous. The school's general liability policy carries a large limit, and tucked inside it is a separate, much smaller cap that applies specifically to abuse claims. The headline limit might be a million or more, while the abuse sublimit is a fraction of that. Many general liability carriers are reluctant to take on this exposure at all, so when they include it, they restrict it to a low figure.

**As an outright exclusion.** Some general liability policies simply carve abuse and molestation out entirely. The coverage that everyone assumes is there is, in plain reading, not there.

**As standalone coverage, or a properly built endorsement.** Here the abuse exposure is insured on its own terms, with meaningful limits, broader policy language, and defense built to handle this specific kind of claim.

The trap is the first one. A school reads "general liability, $1 million" and feels covered for everything underneath it. The abuse sublimit hiding inside might be a small slice of that, and against the size these claims can reach, a small slice can be the difference between an insured event and an institutional collapse.

## What adequate coverage actually looks like

A few features separate real protection from a box that was technically checked.

**Meaningful limits, structured for more than one claimant.** Properly built SAM coverage typically carries both a per-victim limit and an aggregate limit. The per-victim figure applies to each individual claimant. The aggregate is the most the policy will pay across all claims in the period. That structure matters, because these situations can involve more than one person, and a single shared pool can be drained by one severe claim before others are addressed.

**Defense costs that sit outside the limit.** This one is easy to miss and enormously important. When defense costs are paid inside the limit, every dollar spent on lawyers reduces the dollars left to resolve the claim. Given how expensive defending these matters can be, defense inside the limit can quietly consume the coverage. Stronger policies pay defense outside the limit, so the full limit remains available for resolution.

**Attention to how the policy is triggered.** Some SAM coverage is written claims-made, meaning it responds based on when the claim is reported rather than when the event occurred. Some is written occurrence, meaning it responds based on when the event happened, regardless of when the claim eventually surfaces. Because abuse claims are reported so long after the fact, the trigger and, for claims-made policies, the retroactive date are not technicalities. They decide whether a claim about a past period is covered at all. This deserves a deliberate conversation, not a default setting.

## The part that surprises people: the underwriting makes the school safer

Here is the quiet upside of insuring this exposure properly. To write meaningful SAM coverage, an underwriter generally wants to see real abuse-prevention controls in place. So the act of getting good coverage tends to push the school toward the practices that reduce the chance of harm in the first place.

The controls underwriters commonly look for are the same ones child-safety experts recommend:

- Background checks and screening on employees, contractors, and volunteers, including checks against sex-offender registries, refreshed on a regular schedule rather than just at hire.
- A written abuse-prevention policy that everyone is trained on and held to.
- Recurring, documented staff training on recognizing warning signs, maintaining appropriate boundaries, and reporting.
- A two-adult rule, so children are not left alone with a single adult where it can reasonably be avoided.
- Clear reporting procedures that follow mandatory-reporting law, with documentation of how reports are handled.

Schools with stronger controls generally see better terms and pricing, because they genuinely are lower risk. So the coverage and the prevention reinforce each other. Buying the protection seriously tends to make the underlying harm less likely. Few lines of insurance work that way.

## Why the gap is usually the broker's doing

So how does a school end up with a tiny abuse sublimit, or an exclusion, and never know? Almost always the same way.

A generalist broker, the kind who treats a charter school as a small account to process quickly, builds the program off a standard package. The abuse sublimit comes in at whatever the package defaults to, often a low figure, sometimes an exclusion. The broker does not raise it, because raising it means starting a hard, uncomfortable conversation, and the easy-button renewal does not require one. Year after year the policy renews on autopilot. The smallest, most consequential number on the page is never examined, never explained, never benchmarked against what these claims actually cost.

It is not that the school chose to underinsure this risk. It is that nobody put the choice in front of them. The default got accepted because checking it required someone to slow down and bring up the one topic generalists most prefer to skip.

A broker who actually knows schools does the opposite. They name the exposure plainly and respectfully, they pull the policy and read the abuse provisions out loud in plain language, they show the sublimit against what these claims realistically reach, they walk through whether it is sublimit, standalone, or excluded, and they connect the coverage conversation to the school's prevention practices so both get stronger together.

## The question worth sitting with

You do not have to imagine the worst day to take this seriously. You only have to look at one line.

So the question is this. If an abuse allegation surfaced at your school next month, possibly about a period years in the past, do you actually know what limit would respond, whether it sits inside your general liability or stands on its own, whether your defense costs would eat into that limit, and whether anyone has ever weighed that figure against what these claims really cost? Or has that line simply renewed itself, untouched, because it was easier for everyone not to bring it up?

If the honest answer is that you are not sure, you do not have a crisis. You have a line on a page that nobody has read closely. Reading it now, before it is ever tested, is a very different thing than discovering it afterward.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want a careful, respectful second look at how your school's abuse and molestation coverage is actually structured, that is a conversation we are glad to have before anyone ever needs it.*

checklist.content_markdown:
# Company Page Spoke: Sexual Abuse & Molestation Coverage Structure Checklist

*Spoke type: Company storefront page (authoritative, technical, credibility-building). Goal: validate Charter Select to a prospect actively vetting us. Post from the Charter Select company page. Sensitive subject; keep the tone serious and professional. Lead with the matrix, then the checklist.*

---

**Headline:** Your abuse and molestation coverage is usually the smallest number on the policy and the largest claim a school can face. Here is how to read it before you ever need it.

Sexual Abuse and Molestation coverage, often called SAM, protects a charter school against one of the most severe claim types it can face: high in dollar value, frequently reported years after the event, and expensive to defend even when an allegation has no merit. On most school programs this exposure is insured as a small sublimit buried inside general liability, sometimes excluded outright, and rarely benchmarked against what these claims actually cost. Here is how the three structures compare, and the checklist we run on every renewal.

## How SAM coverage is structured, and what each structure means for you

| Structure | What it means | The risk to the school | What to do |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sublimit inside general liability** | A small, separate cap for abuse claims sits underneath the large GL limit | Headline GL limit reads high; the abuse cap is a fraction of it and can be far below claim reality | Find the actual sublimit, benchmark it, decide whether to raise it or move to standalone |
| **Outright exclusion** | The GL policy carves abuse and molestation out entirely | Coverage everyone assumes is there is, in plain reading, absent | Confirm whether an exclusion exists, then secure standalone or endorsed coverage |
| **Standalone coverage or built endorsement** | Abuse exposure insured on its own terms, meaningful limits, broader language | This is the target structure, not the gap | Confirm limits, defense treatment, and trigger are all adequate |

You need to know which of these you actually have, in writing, before a claim ever tests it.

## What adequate coverage looks like

| Feature | Weak version | Adequate version |
|---|---|---|
| **Limit structure** | Single shared pool one severe claim can drain | Both a per-victim limit and an aggregate limit |
| **Defense costs** | Paid inside the limit, eroding funds available to resolve the claim | Paid outside the limit, so the full limit stays available |
| **Trigger and date** | Default setting, never discussed | Claims-made vs occurrence and any retroactive date reviewed deliberately, given how late these claims surface |

## The renewal checklist

- [ ] Locate the abuse and molestation provision and confirm in writing whether it is a sublimit, an exclusion, or standalone coverage.
- [ ] Pull the actual abuse sublimit figure (not the headline GL limit) and read it in plain language.
- [ ] Benchmark that figure against what abuse claims realistically reach, not against the GL limit above it.
- [ ] Confirm whether the policy carries both a per-victim limit and an aggregate limit.
- [ ] Confirm whether defense costs sit inside or outside the limit.
- [ ] Confirm the trigger basis (claims-made or occurrence) and, if claims-made, review the retroactive date.
- [ ] Review prevention controls underwriters look for: background and registry checks refreshed on a schedule, a written abuse-prevention policy, recurring documented staff training, a two-adult rule, and clear mandatory-reporting procedures.
- [ ] Document the confirmed structure, limit, and defense treatment for the board record.

## Why this is standard practice for us and rare elsewhere

A generalist broker building off a standard package lets the abuse sublimit come in at whatever the package defaults to, often a low figure, sometimes an exclusion, and never raises it, because raising it means starting a hard conversation the easy-button renewal does not require. We name this exposure plainly and respectfully, read the abuse provisions out loud at every renewal, show the sublimit against what these claims realistically cost, and connect the coverage to the school's prevention practices so both get stronger together. Properly written, this coverage also tends to make the underlying harm less likely, because the controls underwriters want are the same ones child-safety experts recommend.

**Read the full breakdown:** [LINK TO WHITE PAPER]

topics.title: You Put an Officer on Campus to Make Kids Safer. You May Have Just Bought a Lawsuit Your Policy Won't Touch.
topics.subtitle: A plain-English look at law enforcement legal liability for charter schools with a School Resource Officer, and the gap that opens the moment a sworn officer starts working your hallways.

hub.content_markdown:
# You Put an Officer on Campus to Make Kids Safer. You May Have Just Bought a Lawsuit Your Policy Won't Touch.

*A plain-English look at law enforcement legal liability for charter schools with a School Resource Officer, and the gap that opens the moment a sworn officer starts working your hallways.*

---

A student gets taken to the ground in a hallway. An arm gets twisted. A parent records the last ten seconds on a phone.

Eighteen months later, a federal civil rights complaint names the officer, the police department, and your school.

You call your broker, confident this is what insurance is for. Then you find out the policy you assumed covered it has language that carves law enforcement activity right out.

---

## The decision that quietly changed your risk profile

Bringing a sworn officer onto campus feels like a safety upgrade, and in a lot of ways it is. A School Resource Officer can build relationships, de-escalate, and respond fast when something real happens.

What rarely gets discussed in the same meeting is what an SRO does to your insurance exposure. The moment an armed, sworn officer is acting under color of law inside your building, your school is no longer just an educational operation. It is now adjacent to law enforcement, and law enforcement carries a category of claim that schools almost never see otherwise: use of force, an arrest gone wrong, a search that crossed a line, a detention a court later calls unreasonable. These get litigated as civil rights claims, often under the federal statute that lets people sue government actors for constitutional violations, commonly referenced by its code section, 1983.

Here is the part that catches school leaders flat. The way most SROs are structured does not keep that exposure off your doorstep.

## How SRO arrangements are usually built

There are two common setups, and the distinction matters less than you would hope.

In the most common arrangement, the officer is an employee of a local police department or sheriff's office and gets assigned to your campus under a written agreement. They wear that department's badge, answer to that department's chain of command, and are disciplined through that department.

In the other arrangement, the school employs the officer directly, sometimes through its own security or police function.

You might assume the first setup keeps you clean, since the officer technically works for someone else. It does not, at least not reliably. When an SRO uses force on a student, courts analyze it the same way they would any officer using force on any person, under the same constitutional standard. A plaintiff's attorney looking at that incident does not name only the officer. They name the department, and they name the school, because the officer was operating inside your walls, on your students, in connection with how your school runs. Whether the school ultimately owes anything is a question for the litigation. Getting pulled into the litigation is close to automatic.

So you can be drawn into a law enforcement claim even when you do not employ the officer. That is the exposure. Now look at whether your insurance answers it.

## What your standard policies actually say

A school typically carries two main liability lines. General liability covers ordinary bodily injury and property damage, the slip-and-fall side of running a building. Educators legal liability, often called ELL, covers wrongful acts tied to the educational job: failure to educate, discipline disputes, the professional decisions a school and its board make.

Neither one was built for law enforcement, and the standard forms tend to say so.

General liability is written for the risks of owning and operating a normal business. Law enforcement activity is a recognized professional exposure that gets excluded from these policies, frequently by a specific endorsement that removes coverage for the named insured's law enforcement activities. ELL has its own set of exclusions, and claims arising from intentional or criminal acts, the territory a force or false-arrest claim lives in, often sit outside it.

Read those two facts together and the gap comes into focus. The two policies you rely on most are each drafted to push law enforcement claims away. A use-of-force suit can land squarely in the seam between them, where neither one was designed to respond.

This is the line that built the problem: the coverage you assume protects you against an SRO incident is usually the exact coverage written to exclude it.

## The coverage that was actually built for this

The line designed for this exposure is Law Enforcement Legal Liability, sometimes shortened to LELL or sold as police professional liability.

It exists precisely because general liability shuts law enforcement out. LELL is built to respond to claims arising from law enforcement activities: excessive or improper use of force, wrongful arrest or detention, improper search and seizure, and the civil rights claims that get filed under that 1983 framework. It typically funds defense costs, which in a federal civil rights case can run high long before anyone reaches a verdict or settlement.

Like any line, it has its own limits and carve-outs. Coverage for the conduct itself generally does not extend to truly willful, wanton, or criminal acts, and these policies commonly exclude punitive damages. The specifics vary by carrier and by how the policy is written, so the structure matters and a careful read matters more. The headline, though, is straightforward. If a sworn officer is working your campus, this is the line that was engineered for the claim, and it is usually not riding along inside the policies you already have.

## The document everyone signs and nobody coordinates

When the officer belongs to a police department, the relationship runs through a written agreement, usually a Memorandum of Understanding, an MOU. This is the single most important risk document in the whole arrangement, and it routinely gets signed without the one person who should read it ever seeing it.

A well-built MOU spells out the officer's role, who supervises them, how complaints get handled, and, critically, how responsibility and insurance get allocated between the school and the department. The common pattern is that each party stays responsible for the acts of its own people, with each side agreeing, to the extent the law allows, to indemnify the other for claims arising from its own conduct. Many MOUs also state who has to carry insurance and at what level.

Sit with what that means. The MOU can obligate your school to indemnify the department in certain situations, or require your school to carry insurance for this exposure, and those obligations are only as good as the coverage standing behind them. A school administrator and a police chief sign the document. The school's broker, the person who would know whether the indemnity promise is backed by an actual policy, is often never in the room. The agreement gets executed, the officer reports for duty, and the insurance side of the deal stays an open question until a claim tests it.

Every path to this exposure runs through one place: the agreement that put the officer on campus and the policies that are supposed to answer for what the officer does. If those two are never lined up against each other, you do not have a program. You have a signature and a hope.

## Where the generalist broker let this slip

A broker who treats charter schools as small accounts to renew quickly is not reading your MOU. They were not at the table when it was signed, they did not ask whether a sworn officer now works your halls, and they renewed your general liability and ELL the same way they renew everyone's, without a thought about the law enforcement exclusion sitting inside both.

That is how the gap forms. Not through some exotic failure, but through the ordinary inattention of a broker who never asked the one question that changes the analysis: is there an officer on this campus, and if so, what happens when a claim comes out of what that officer does?

A broker who actually knows schools asks it on the first call. They want to know whether you have an SRO, how the arrangement is structured, and whether there is an MOU. They read that MOU against your policies. They check what your general liability and ELL forms exclude. And if there is a real law enforcement exposure with no line built to catch it, they tell you plainly, before a parent's phone ever starts recording, instead of after.

## The question worth sitting with

You made a deliberate choice to put a trained officer on your campus. That was a real decision about safety, and it deserves a matching decision about risk.

So here is the one worth asking yourself, today, while it is still a quiet question and not a lawsuit:

If an incident involving your officer turned into a civil rights claim next month, do you actually know which policy responds, and have you confirmed it isn't one of the two that are written to exclude it? Or has nobody on your side ever lined up the MOU you signed against the coverage you're paying for?

If you can't answer that with confidence, you don't have a coverage problem yet. You have a question no one has checked. The check is a conversation. The cost of skipping it shows up on the day you can least afford a surprise.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you have an officer on campus and want a second set of eyes on your MOU and your policies before a claim tests them, that is exactly the kind of review we do before anyone asks.*

---

**Headline:** You put an officer on campus to keep kids safe. The two policies you trust most are both written to walk away from what happens next.

---

A school leader I talked with last year had done the responsible thing. She put a trained, sworn officer on her campus. A real safety decision, the kind a board feels good signing off on.

Then a hallway incident happened. A student taken to the ground, an arm twisted, a parent recording the last ten seconds on a phone. Eighteen months later a federal civil rights complaint landed, and it named the officer, the police department, and her school.

I grew up around manual labor and a ranch, where doing a job halfway meant somebody got hurt or something broke, and you owned it. That is the standard I carried into insurance. It is not the standard a lot of charter schools are getting, and the School Resource Officer gap is one of the cleanest examples I know.

Here is the mechanic, in plain English.

A school usually carries two main liability lines. General liability handles the slip-and-fall side of running a building. Educators legal liability, ELL, handles the professional decisions a school and its board make. Both are useful. Neither one was built for law enforcement, and the standard forms tend to say so out loud.

ELL has its own exclusions, and claims tied to intentional or criminal acts, which is the ground a use-of-force or false-arrest claim stands on, frequently sit outside it.

Read those two facts together and the gap comes into focus. The coverage you assume protects you against an officer incident is usually the exact coverage written to exclude it. A use-of-force suit can land right in the seam between the two policies, where neither one was designed to answer.

People assume that if the officer technically works for the police department, the school stays clean. It does not work that way reliably. Whether the school ultimately owes anything is a question for the litigation. Getting pulled into the litigation is close to automatic.

There is a line built for exactly this. Law Enforcement Legal Liability, sometimes called LELL or police professional liability. It exists precisely because general liability shuts law enforcement out. It responds to use-of-force claims, wrongful arrest, improper search, and the civil rights claims filed under that federal framework, and it funds the defense costs that pile up long before anyone reaches a verdict.

And there is one document that ties the whole thing together: the Memorandum of Understanding, the MOU, between the school and the department. It spells out who supervises the officer, how complaints get handled, and how insurance and indemnity get allocated. It is the single most important risk document in the arrangement, and it routinely gets signed without the one person who should read it ever seeing it. An administrator and a police chief sign. The broker, the person who would know whether the indemnity promise is backed by an actual policy, is usually never in the room.

The reason this gets missed is not that it is exotic. It is that a generalist broker treating your school as a quick renewal never asked the one question that changes everything: is there an officer on this campus, and what happens when a claim comes out of what that officer does? A broker who actually knows schools asks it on the first call, reads the MOU against your policies, and tells you plainly if there is a real exposure with no line built to catch it. That is most of why Charter Select only works with charter schools and reads the whole policy every renewal.

I wrote the full breakdown up on our site. No jargon, no gate, free to read and free to forward to your board: **[LINK TO WHITE PAPER]**

Or has nobody on your side ever lined up the MOU you signed against the coverage you are paying for?

If you cannot answer that with confidence, you do not have a coverage problem yet. You have a question no one has checked.

Aaron Schwen

checklist.label: Company-page checklist / matrix
checklist.content_markdown:
# Company Page Spoke: School Resource Officer Coverage Gap Checklist

*Spoke type: Company storefront page (authoritative, technical, credibility-building). Goal: validate Charter Select to a prospect actively vetting us. Post from the Charter Select company page. Lead with the matrix, then the checklist.*

---

**Headline:** Have a School Resource Officer on campus? The two policies you carry most are usually written to exclude exactly the claim an officer creates.

The moment a sworn officer starts working your hallways, your school sits adjacent to law enforcement, and law enforcement carries a category of claim schools almost never see otherwise: use of force, wrongful arrest, improper search, detention a court later calls unreasonable. These get litigated as federal civil rights claims. Below is how your standard lines respond to that exposure, how the purpose-built line responds, and the checklist we run for any school with an officer on campus.

## How each policy line treats a law enforcement claim

| Coverage line | Built for | How it treats an SRO use-of-force / civil rights claim |
|---|---|---|
| **General Liability (GL)** | Ordinary bodily injury and property damage from operating a building | Law enforcement activity is a recognized professional exposure, commonly removed by a specific endorsement that excludes the named insured's law enforcement activities |
| **Educators Legal Liability (ELL)** | Wrongful acts tied to the educational job: failure to educate, discipline disputes, board and professional decisions | Claims arising from intentional or criminal acts, the territory of a force or false-arrest claim, often sit outside the policy |
| **Law Enforcement Legal Liability (LELL)** | Claims arising from law enforcement activities | Responds to excessive force, wrongful arrest or detention, improper search and seizure, and civil rights (1983) claims; typically funds defense costs. Note its own carve-outs: willful/criminal conduct and punitive damages are commonly excluded |

GL and ELL are each drafted to push law enforcement claims away. A use-of-force suit lands in the seam between them. LELL is the line engineered to catch it, and it is usually not riding along inside the policies you already have.

## The SRO coverage checklist

- [ ] Confirm whether a sworn officer works any of your campuses (department-assigned or school-employed).
- [ ] Locate the Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) for any department-assigned officer and read it in full.
- [ ] In the MOU, identify the indemnity language: who indemnifies whom, and for whose conduct.
- [ ] In the MOU, identify any requirement that the school carry insurance for this exposure, and at what limit.
- [ ] Pull your GL form and confirm whether a law enforcement activities exclusion endorsement is attached.
- [ ] Pull your ELL form and confirm how it treats intentional and criminal acts.
- [ ] Determine whether Law Enforcement Legal Liability (LELL / police professional) coverage is in place anywhere in the program.
- [ ] If LELL is required by the MOU or by the exposure and is absent, price and place it before the next incident, not after.
- [ ] Confirm the MOU's insurance obligations are actually backed by bound coverage, in writing.
- [ ] Document the confirmed structure for the board record.

## Why this is standard practice for us and rare elsewhere

A broker who treats charter schools as small accounts to renew quickly does not read your MOU, was not at the table when it was signed, and renews your GL and ELL without a thought about the law enforcement exclusion sitting inside both. We ask whether there is an officer on campus on the first call, read the MOU against your policies, and confirm whether the line built for this exposure is actually in force, because a federal civil rights claim is the worst possible moment to discover the seam.

**Read the full breakdown:** [LINK TO WHITE PAPER]

---

*Posting notes: this is the credibility asset. Keep it clean and technical. The checklist also works as a downloadable PDF lead-capture on the website (top/bottom of the hub page). Replace [LINK TO WHITE PAPER] with the live URL.*

topics.title: Your Property Insurance Needs a Broken Window to Pay. The Worst Day at a School Doesn't Come With One.
topics.subtitle: Why a violent event on campus can cost a school everything and still trigger no property claim, and the coverage built specifically to respond when there's no physical damage to point at.

hub.content_markdown:
# Your Property Insurance Needs a Broken Window to Pay. The Worst Day at a School Doesn't Come With One.

*Why a violent event on campus can cost a school everything and still trigger no property claim, and the coverage built specifically to respond when there's no physical damage to point at.*

---

The unthinkable happens on your campus. There's no fire. No flood. No broken glass, no caved roof, nothing a camera would call damage.

In the weeks after, the costs come anyway. Counselors and trauma care. Round-the-clock security nobody planned for. Families who need support. A crisis communications team. And the quiet one that breaks the budget: students who don't come back, in a school funded by the students who show up.

You file a claim on your property policy. The adjuster asks where the physical damage is. There isn't any. Claim closed.

Here's why the policy you'd reach for can't help, and what actually does.

---

## Property coverage runs on physical damage. That's the whole catch.

A standard commercial property policy, and the business interruption coverage attached to it, is built around one trigger: direct physical loss or damage to the property. A fire, a burst pipe, a storm that tears off part of the roof. Something physically happened to the building, so the policy responds, and the business interruption piece replaces the income you lost while you repaired it.

That structure works fine for a flood. It falls apart for an act of violence.

A violent event on a campus can be the most devastating thing that ever happens to a school and cause almost no qualifying physical damage to the building. The harm is human, operational, and reputational. The policy that's supposed to keep you running after a disaster is looking for a kind of damage that this disaster didn't produce, so it stays shut.

## The bill that arrives after a violent event

Set aside, for a moment, the human weight of it, which no policy can address. Look only at the costs that land on the school as an organization, because those are the ones insurance is supposed to catch.

After a violent incident, a school typically faces some mix of:

- **Crisis management and communications.** Press, community, families, public officials, all at once, all immediately.
- **Psychiatric and medical care** for students, staff, and families, often for a long time.
- **Security upgrades and on-site presence** that didn't exist in the budget the week before.
- **Counseling and family support** at a scale a school has never staffed for.
- **Relocation or remediation** of the space, because sometimes people cannot walk back into the room.
- **Lost enrollment**, which for a charter school isn't a soft metric. It's the funding.

That last one deserves its own paragraph.

## Why this lands harder on a school than almost any other organization

Charter schools live and die on enrollment. Funding follows the student. When attendance drops, when families transfer out, when next year's enrollment softens because of what happened, that is a direct hit to the dollars that keep the doors open and the staff paid.

A normal business takes a violent event as a tragedy and a disruption. A charter school takes it as a tragedy, a disruption, and a funding cliff at the same time, with no corporate risk department and no cash cushion to ride it out. The very thing that makes the model work, money that follows students, becomes the thing that bleeds when students stop coming.

So the school carrying only standard property and liability is exposed exactly where it's weakest, and the coverage it assumed would respond is asking about broken windows.

## What actually responds: coverage built for the event itself

There's a category of coverage written specifically for this. It goes by names like active assailant, deadly weapon protection, or active shooter coverage, and specialty carriers now write it as a standalone policy.

The thing that makes it different is the trigger. It does not wait for physical damage to the building. It responds to the violent event itself. That single design choice is the whole point, because it's the reason it pays where your property policy can't.

A well-built policy in this category typically funds the costs that actually show up: crisis management and PR, medical and psychiatric care for victims, extra security, counseling, business interruption from the drop in attendance and enrollment, and remediation or relocation of the space. It puts money in motion in the days after, when a school has the least capacity to figure out funding and the most need for it.

## Reading one of these correctly matters

This is coverage where the fine print decides whether it's real, so a few things are worth confirming rather than assuming:

**The trigger definition.** Does it respond only to an actual event, or also to a credible threat that forces a closure and a response? The broader trigger covers more of what schools actually live through.

**What's inside.** Confirm it includes business interruption tied to enrollment and attendance, not just immediate crisis costs. For a charter school, the enrollment piece is the part that matters most and the part most likely to be thin.

**The limits, against the real cost.** Crisis response, long-term care, and a multi-month enrollment dip add up fast. A small limit is a gesture, not a safety net.

## Before you assume you're covered for this

You don't need to dwell on the scenario to answer the question that matters. You just need to know what your current policies actually do.

So here's the one to sit with:

If a violent event happened on your campus tomorrow and caused no physical damage to the building, which of your current policies pays for the counselors, the security, the crisis team, and the enrollment you'd lose over the following year? If the honest answer is "I'm not sure any of them do," then the gap isn't theoretical. It's the one your current broker never brought up.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want to know exactly what your current coverage would and wouldn't do on the worst possible day, that's a conversation we'd rather have now than after.*

newsletter.content_markdown:
# LinkedIn Personal Newsletter: Active Assailant Coverage

*Spoke type: Personal profile newsletter (Aaron's voice). Goal: human story + compressed insight, driving to the full white paper. Post from Aaron's personal LinkedIn. ~750 words.*

---

**Headline:** The worst day a school can have doesn't break a window. So the policy built to respond to a disaster never opens.

---

A while back I sat with a school leader who walked me through what her year would look like if the unthinkable happened on her campus. Not because she expected it. Because a board member had finally asked the question out loud, and nobody in the room could answer it.

We didn't dwell on the event itself. We looked at the bill that arrives after. The counselors. The round-the-clock security that wasn't in any budget the week before. The families who need support. The crisis communications team. And the quiet one that I watched land on her, the one that changes the math for a charter school specifically: the students who don't come back.

Then she asked me which of her policies would pay for all of that. I told her the honest answer, which is the reason I'm writing this.

Her standard property policy, the one most schools assume is the safety net, almost certainly would not pay a dime of it.

Here's the mechanic, in plain English.

A commercial property policy, and the business interruption coverage stapled to it, runs on one trigger: direct physical loss or damage to the building. A fire. A burst pipe. A storm that peels back the roof. Something physically happened to the property, so the policy responds and the business interruption piece replaces the income you lost while you repaired it.

That structure works fine for a flood. It falls apart for an act of violence.

A violent event on a campus can be the most devastating thing that ever happens to a school and cause almost no qualifying physical damage. The harm is human, operational, and reputational. The adjuster shows up, asks where the physical damage is, and there isn't any. So the policy that was supposed to keep the school running stays shut, looking for a kind of damage this disaster never produced.

I grew up around manual labor and a ranch, where if you did a job halfway, somebody got hurt or something broke, and you owned it. That's the standard I expected when I got into insurance. Telling a school leader after the fact that her disaster coverage doesn't recognize her disaster is the opposite of that standard. It's the part I can't stomach, and it's most of why Charter Select exists.

Now, why this lands harder on a school than on almost any other organization. Charter schools live and die on enrollment. The funding follows the student. When attendance drops, when families transfer out, when next year's enrollment softens because of what happened, that is a direct hit to the dollars that keep the doors open and the staff paid. A normal business takes a violent event as a tragedy and a disruption. A charter school takes it as a tragedy, a disruption, and a funding cliff at the same time, with no corporate risk department and no cash cushion to ride it out.

There is coverage written specifically for this. It goes by names like active assailant, deadly weapon protection, or active shooter coverage, and specialty carriers now write it as a standalone policy. The thing that makes it different is the trigger. It doesn't wait for physical damage. It responds to the violent event itself. That single design choice is the whole point, because it's the reason it pays where your property policy can't.

A well-built version of it funds the costs that actually show up: crisis management and PR, medical and psychiatric care for victims, extra security, counseling, business interruption tied to the drop in attendance and enrollment, and remediation or relocation of the space. It puts money in motion in the days after, when a school has the least capacity to figure out funding and the most need for it.

I wrote the full breakdown up on our site, no jargon, no gate, free to read and free to forward to your board or your business manager: **[LINK TO WHITE PAPER]**

If you only do one thing with this, sit with one question.

If a violent event happened on your campus tomorrow and caused no physical damage to the building, which of your current policies pays for the counselors, the security, the crisis team, and the enrollment you'd lose over the following year? If the honest answer is "I'm not sure any of them do," then the gap isn't theoretical. It's the one your current broker never brought up.

Forward this to the school leader who needs it.

Aaron Schwen

---

*Posting notes: replace [LINK TO WHITE PAPER] with the live hub URL. Keep the sign-off personal, and keep the tone grounded, not dramatic. First comment should drop the link again (LinkedIn suppresses posts with outbound links in the body, so some people put the link in the first comment instead, test both).*

checklist.label: Company-page checklist / matrix
checklist.content_markdown:
# Company Page Spoke: Active Assailant Coverage Gap Checklist

*Spoke type: Company storefront page (authoritative, technical, credibility-building). Goal: validate Charter Select to a prospect actively vetting us. Post from the Charter Select company page. Lead with the matrix, then the checklist.*

---

**Headline:** A violent event on campus can cost a school everything and trigger no property claim. Here is which policy actually responds, and what to confirm before you assume you're covered.

Most school risk programs are built around physical damage. A standard property policy, and the business interruption coverage attached to it, only responds when something physically happens to the building. A violent event often causes catastrophic human and operational loss with little or no qualifying physical damage, so the policy stays shut. Below is how the relevant coverages compare on the trigger that matters, and the checklist we run when we review a school's exposure to a violent event.

## How the coverage responds to a no-physical-damage event

| Coverage | Trigger | Pays for crisis costs, care, lost enrollment? | Fits a violent event with no physical damage? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Commercial property** | Direct physical loss or damage to the building | No | No. No physical damage, no claim. |
| **Business interruption (attached to property)** | Income loss following a covered physical loss | No, the income loss must trace to physical damage | No. Enrollment drop without physical damage isn't a trigger. |
| **General liability** | Third-party bodily injury or property damage claims against the school | Defense and damages on covered claims only, not the school's own response costs | Partial and indirect. Doesn't fund crisis response, security, or lost enrollment. |
| **Active assailant / deadly weapon coverage** | The violent event itself, no physical damage required | Yes, built for exactly these costs | Yes. This is the coverage designed for the event. |

The single design difference that matters: active assailant coverage responds to the violent event, not to damage to the building. That is why it pays where the property program can't.

## The coverage review checklist

- [ ] Confirm whether the program carries any standalone active assailant / deadly weapon coverage at all, or only property and liability.
- [ ] Read the trigger definition: does it respond only to an actual event, or also to a credible threat that forces a closure and a response?
- [ ] Confirm the policy includes business interruption tied to enrollment and attendance, not just immediate crisis costs.
- [ ] Confirm covered cost categories: crisis management and PR, medical and psychiatric care, extra security, counseling and family support, remediation or relocation.
- [ ] Test the limit against the real cost: multi-month crisis response, long-term care, and a sustained enrollment dip, not a token figure.
- [ ] Confirm what the existing property and BI policies would and would not pay for a no-physical-damage event, in writing.
- [ ] Document the findings for the board record so the gap is a decision, not an accident.

## Why this is standard practice for us and rare elsewhere

A generalist broker places property and liability, calls the program complete, and never tests it against the one event that produces no broken window. We work charter schools only, we read the full program, and we pressure-test it against the no-physical-damage event specifically, because that is the scenario where a school is most exposed and least able to absorb the cost. The enrollment piece is the part most likely to be thin, and for a charter school it's the part that decides whether the doors stay open.

**Read the full breakdown:** [LINK TO WHITE PAPER]

---

topics.title: The Disaster Blueprint: Why One School Reopens in Three Weeks and the One Next Door Fights Its Carrier for a Year
topics.subtitle: A pre-loss claims-readiness playbook for charter schools. The claim you win is the one you prepared for before the building ever flooded.

hub.content_markdown:
# The Disaster Blueprint: Why One School Reopens in Three Weeks and the One Next Door Fights Its Carrier for a Year

*A pre-loss claims-readiness playbook for charter schools. The claim you win is the one you prepared for before the building ever flooded.*

---

Two schools take the same storm. Same county, same week, same kind of water through the same kind of roof.

One is back in session in under a month. The other is still arguing with its insurer the following spring, classes scattered across a borrowed church basement and two trailers, payroll bleeding, families pulling kids.

Same damage. Same coverage on paper. The difference between those two outcomes was decided long before the wind picked up.

---

## What actually separates them

People assume the fast recovery is about the policy, that one school bought better coverage. Sometimes that's true. More often, both schools had adequate policies and only one was ready to use it.

A property claim is a documentation contest. The carrier doesn't pay because the building is wrecked. It pays because you can prove what you had, prove what it was worth, prove the damage, and prove you did your part to limit it. The school that hands all of that over in the first week gets paid in the first month. The school reconstructing its records from memory while the mold spreads gets a fight.

You build the winning side of that contest before the loss, or you don't build it at all. After the alarm goes off is too late to start photographing equipment that's already underwater.

So here is the blueprint. None of it is expensive, and most of it is an afternoon of work you can start this week.

## 1. A current asset and property inventory, with photos and values

This is the foundation, and it's the one almost everyone skips.

You need a running list of what the school owns: furniture, technology, kitchen and HVAC equipment, science and shop gear, instruments, library and curriculum materials, playground and athletic equipment, anything bolted down and anything that isn't. For each significant item or category, capture a photo, a rough age, and a value. Serial numbers on the expensive electronics. Receipts where you have them.

Walk every room with a phone, closets and storage areas included. Video works too, narrate as you go. Then redo it once a year, because the inventory you took three years ago doesn't include the forty Chromebooks you bought last fall.

When a fire takes a wing of the building, the adjuster will ask what was in it. "A lot of expensive stuff" is not a number they can cut a check against. A dated photo and a value is.

## 2. Policies and the declarations page, stored off-site

Your insurance documents cannot live only in a filing cabinet inside the building they're insuring. If the building burns, so do they.

Keep a full copy of every policy, and especially the declarations page, somewhere the disaster can't reach. A cloud drive. A personal email to yourself. A folder your broker holds for you. The dec page is the one-page summary of what you bought: limits, deductibles, named insureds, and the coverage forms attached. In the first chaotic hours after a loss, that page tells you what you're working with.

Put the claims phone number and your broker's direct line in that same folder. When the office is taped off, you don't want to be hunting for a number on a sticky note inside it.

## 3. Know your deductibles and sublimits before you need them

This is where schools get blindsided, and it's pure homework you can do on a quiet afternoon.

Pull the dec page and read three things.

Your deductible. The amount you eat before coverage kicks in. Property deductibles can be a flat dollar figure, but for wind, hail, and named storms many policies use a percentage of the building's insured value instead, which can be a much larger number than people expect. Know which kind you have.

Your sublimits. A policy can carry a healthy overall limit while quietly capping specific things far lower. Flood and water backup, debris removal, certain types of equipment, and other categories can each have their own ceiling buried in the forms. A sublimit is the most you'll get for that category no matter how big your total limit looks.

Your extra expense and business income terms, if you have them. These are what fund the trailers, the temporary lease, the generator rental that keeps you operating while the building gets rebuilt. Knowing what's there before the loss tells you how fast you can move.

If you can't explain your wind deductible and your top three sublimits in plain language right now, that's the assignment for this week.

## 4. A documented contact tree

In the first hour after a loss, confusion costs you. People stand around assuming someone else made the call. Three different staff give three different stories to a reporter. Nobody starts the photos.

Write down, on one page, who does what:

- Who calls the broker and the carrier, and who is the backup if that person is unreachable.
- Who documents the damage with photos and video before anything gets touched.
- Who handles arranging emergency mitigation, the board-up, the water extraction, the tarp.
- Who is the single voice to families and to media. One voice. Everyone else routes to them.
- Who pulls the off-site policy folder and inventory.

Put names and cell numbers on it. Keep a copy off-site with the policies. A loss is going to happen on a Saturday or at 2 a.m., not during a calm staff meeting, and the plan only helps if it exists before the phone rings.

## 5. Mitigate immediately, because the policy requires it

Here is a piece of the contract most school leaders have never been told. Your property policy obligates you to take reasonable steps to protect the property from further damage after a loss. It's a standard duty, and ignoring it can reduce or jeopardize what the carrier pays.

In plain terms: if a pipe bursts, you're expected to shut the water and get the standing water out, not let it sit for a week and then bill the carrier for the mold that grew because you waited. Tarp the open roof. Board the broken window. Move the salvageable equipment to dry ground.

Two things make this work. First, keep the receipts, reasonable emergency mitigation is itself usually a covered cost. Second, and this is the part people miss, document the damage before you start cleaning it up. Mitigate fast, but photograph first.

## 6. Document everything, during and after

The cleanup instinct is strong and it works against you. Once the wreckage is hauled to a dumpster, your proof goes with it.

Photograph and video the damage from every angle before anyone moves anything. Keep damaged property where it is until the adjuster has seen it or has told you in writing it's fine to discard. Start a simple loss log the day it happens: a running record of what occurred, who you spoke with, what they said, and what you spent. Keep every receipt, the mitigation crew, the temporary equipment, the extra staff hours, all of it.

That log is boring to keep and decisive to have. Months later, when the details have gone fuzzy and the claim is in negotiation, the school with the contemporaneous record is the one whose version holds up.

## 7. Understand the timeline and the proof of loss

A property claim runs on a clock, and the clock has your name on it, not the carrier's.

Most policies require prompt notice of a loss, so report it quickly even before you know the full scope. Many then require a sworn proof of loss, a formal document where you state the amount you're claiming, typically due within a set window after the carrier requests it. There are deadlines for submitting your documentation and, often, time limits on how long you have to sue if it comes to that. Missing these isn't a paperwork hiccup. It can be the carrier's clean reason to deny.

You don't have to memorize the windows. You have to know they exist and have someone watching them, which is exactly what the next section is about.

## Where the generalist broker leaves you standing alone

Most of what's above never gets mentioned to a school because the broker who sold the policy treats the account like a renewal to process, not a client to protect. They emailed the dec page once, two years ago, buried in a PDF nobody opened. They never explained the wind deductible or the flood sublimit, and they certainly never walked the building to talk about an inventory.

Then the loss happens, and the difference shows. The generalist is hard to reach, slow to call back, and absent from the room where the adjuster decides what your damage is worth. They didn't prepare you before, and they don't advocate for you during. You're negotiating a six-figure claim alone, against a professional who does this every day, with records you're rebuilding from memory.

A broker who actually does the job builds this blueprint with you on a calm Tuesday. They make sure you understand your deductibles and sublimits before a storm tests them. And when the loss comes, they show up, push back on a lowball adjustment, and keep the timeline and the proof of loss from quietly running out on you. That presence at claim time is the whole reason a broker exists. It's also the moment the cheap, absent ones vanish.

## The question worth sitting with

Picture the worst Tuesday. A fire, a flood, a storm that opens the roof. Tonight.

Could you, tomorrow morning, hand someone a current inventory of what the building held, a copy of your policy from outside the building, and a one-page plan of who does what? Or would the honest answer be that you'd be starting all of it from scratch, in the wreckage, on the worst day your school has had?

Because that gap, between what you could prove today and what you'd wish you'd done, is the exact gap your carrier will price the claim inside of. Better to close it on a quiet afternoon than discover it the morning after.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want a second set of eyes on your property coverage and a real disaster blueprint built before you ever need it, that's the kind of work we'd rather do now than during the cleanup.*

checklist.content_markdown:
# Company Page Spoke: The Pre-Loss Disaster Blueprint Readiness Checklist

*Spoke type: Company storefront page (authoritative, technical, credibility-building). Goal: validate Charter Select to a prospect actively vetting us. Post from the Charter Select company page. Lead with the matrix, then the checklist.*

---

**Headline:** A property claim is a documentation contest you win before the loss, or you do not win it at all. Here is the readiness most schools discover they are missing the morning after.

When disaster hits a charter school, the carrier does not pay because the building is wrecked. It pays because the school can prove what it owned, what it was worth, the extent of the damage, and that reasonable steps were taken to limit it. The school that hands that proof over in week one gets paid in month one. The school reconstructing records from memory gets a year-long fight. Below is what "ready" actually looks like, and the checklist we build with every school before a storm ever tests it.

## Ready vs. unprepared, item by item

| Readiness element | Prepared school | Unprepared school |
|---|---|---|
| **Asset inventory** | Current, photographed, valued, refreshed yearly | Reconstructed from memory while the wreckage sits in a dumpster |
| **Policy & dec page** | Stored off-site, retrievable in the first hour | Locked inside the taped-off building it was insuring |
| **Deductibles & sublimits** | Known in plain language before the loss | Discovered after the loss, often a percentage-of-value surprise |
| **Contact tree** | One page, named roles, cell numbers, off-site copy | "Someone should call the carrier," nobody does |
| **Mitigation duty** | Documented, receipts kept, damage photographed first | Standing water sits, mold spreads, payment shrinks |
| **Loss log & timeline** | Contemporaneous record from day one, deadlines watched | Fuzzy recollection, missed proof-of-loss window |

You do not need every box perfect to recover well. You do need the foundation built before the alarm goes off, because none of it can be created after the building floods.

## The pre-loss readiness checklist

- [ ] Build a current asset and property inventory: photos, rough age, and value for every significant item or category, serial numbers on the expensive electronics.
- [ ] Walk every room, closet, and storage area; capture a narrated video as a backup record.
- [ ] Refresh the inventory once a year so recent purchases are actually in it.
- [ ] Store a full copy of every policy and the declarations page off-site (cloud drive, email, broker-held folder).
- [ ] Keep the claims phone number and broker's direct line in that same off-site folder.
- [ ] Read and document your deductibles, including any percentage-based wind or named-storm deductible.
- [ ] Identify your top sublimits (flood, water backup, debris removal, equipment categories) and what they cap.
- [ ] Confirm your extra expense and business income terms, the coverage that funds trailers and temporary leases.
- [ ] Write a one-page contact tree: who calls the carrier, who documents, who mitigates, who is the single voice to families and media, who pulls the off-site folder.
- [ ] Confirm the policy's mitigation duty is understood: protect the property from further damage, keep receipts, photograph before cleaning.
- [ ] Start a loss log the day a loss occurs and keep every receipt.
- [ ] Note the prompt-notice and proof-of-loss deadlines, and assign someone to watch the clock.

## Why this is standard practice for us and rare elsewhere

A generalist broker chasing a renewal emails the dec page once and disappears until the next invoice. We build the disaster blueprint with a school on a calm Tuesday, confirm the deductibles and sublimits in plain language, and show up at claim time to push back on a lowball adjustment and keep the proof-of-loss timeline from quietly running out. Presence at the loss is the entire reason a broker exists, and it is the part the cheap ones skip.

**Read the full breakdown:** [LINK TO WHITE PAPER]

---
