hub.content_markdown:
# The Health Plan Where a Healthy Year Pays You Back

*A plain-English look at level-funded plans and group captives: the funding models that return surplus to the school instead of the insurer, what they actually require, and the honest risk nobody should hide from you.*

---

There's a version of your school's health plan where a healthy year ends with money coming back to your budget instead of vanishing into the insurer's profit.

It isn't exotic. It isn't only for big corporations. Schools your size run on it right now. And the reason most charter leaders have never seriously looked at it is that their generalist broker found it easier to renew the same fully insured plan every year and pass along the increase.

Here's how the alternatives actually work, who each one fits, and the part of the pitch most people leave out: the real risk.

---

## The spectrum, in plain terms

Health plan funding runs along a spectrum, and fully insured sits at one end of it.

At that end, you pay a fixed premium, the insurer takes all the risk, and the insurer keeps any surplus. Predictable, and built so you never share in a good year.

At the far other end is full self-funding, where the employer pays claims directly and keeps the savings when claims are low. It offers the most control and the most upside, but on its own it carries real volatility, and it doesn't work well for a smaller employer, because a single catastrophic claim can blow a hole in the budget. Self-funding relies on having enough covered lives to make the averages stable, and most individual schools don't have that on their own.

Between those two ends sit the two models worth a charter school's attention: level-funded plans and group captives.

## Level-funded: the step off fully insured, with a safety net

A level-funded plan is the practical first move away from fully insured, and it's built for exactly the small-to-midsize employer a charter school usually is.

You pay a set monthly amount, which keeps the budget predictability you're used to. That payment splits into three parts: a claims fund to pay your people's medical claims, the administrative fee, and the premium for stop-loss insurance. Stop-loss is the safety net. It's a policy that caps your exposure, so if claims run high, on a single large claim or across the whole group, stop-loss absorbs the overage and your downside is limited to what you already budgeted.

Now the upside that fully insured never gave you: if your group's actual claims come in below the funded amount, you get the surplus back as a refund, typically within a few months of the plan year closing. A healthy year finally pays you instead of the carrier.

There's a second benefit that's easy to overlook and genuinely valuable. Level-funded plans give you real monthly claims data, things like emergency room usage, prescription patterns, and cost drivers. For the first time you can see what's actually moving your costs and manage it, instead of being handed a renewal number with no explanation.

Two honest caveats. Level-funded plans carry more compliance and reporting responsibility than fully insured, and they aren't available in every state because of how stop-loss is regulated. A competent broker handles the first and checks the second before recommending anything.

## Group captives: for bigger schools, or schools willing to band together

A group captive is the next level of control, and it's where the model gets interesting for the charter world specifically.

A captive is an insurance company owned by the employers in it. Instead of buying coverage from a carrier that keeps the profit, a group of like-sized employers pool together and effectively insure themselves, with structure around it so no single member carries the whole risk. It usually works in three layers. Each employer self-funds its own routine, predictable claims up to a set point. Above that, a shared middle layer pools the mid-sized claims across all the member employers, so one member's rough year is cushioned by the group. Above that, reinsurance catches the truly catastrophic claims.

The payoff: a large share of what used to be fixed premium becomes variable cost, and the dollars that aren't spent on claims come back to the members as dividends rather than staying with an insurer. Members also get deep claims data, get underwritten on their own performance instead of being lumped into an opaque pool, and gain real negotiating leverage with administrators and pharmacy managers.

The charter-specific angle is the one worth sitting with. A single school may be too small to self-fund alone. But charter schools share a risk profile, and banding together into a captive is exactly the kind of pooling that lets smaller organizations reach a scale none of them could reach on their own. The model is built for groups of similar employers who want control without carrying the full risk solo.

## The honest risk, said out loud

Any broker who pitches these as free money is lying to you, so here's the straight version.

These models move some risk from the insurer onto the school. In a bad claims year, you will pay more than your best case, up to the point where stop-loss or the captive's reinsurance takes over. That ceiling is defined and known in advance, which is the whole point of the structure, but it is higher than the flat premium of a fully insured plan in a bad year. The tradeoff you're making is accepting a defined, capped downside in exchange for keeping the upside in a good year and getting data you can actually act on.

They also demand more from your broker. These plans live or die on the claims analysis, the stop-loss contract terms, and ongoing management. A broker who can't or won't do that work has no business putting you in one. That's the real reason a generalist defaults to fully insured: it requires nothing of them.

## Before you renew the same plan one more time

You don't have to commit to anything to find out whether this math works for your school. You need someone to run your actual numbers, not hand you a generic pitch.

So here's the question worth asking:

Has anyone ever run your school's real claims history against a level-funded or captive model to show you, in dollars, what a healthy year would have returned to your budget instead of the insurer's? If the honest answer is no, you've been renewing the most expensive version of this without ever seeing the alternative.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. We run the funding analysis on your real claims data before recommending a single change, including the downside, so you're deciding with the whole picture in front of you.*

newsletter.content_markdown:
# LinkedIn Personal Newsletter: The Health Plan That Pays You Back

*Spoke type: Personal profile newsletter (Aaron's voice). Goal: human story + compressed insight, driving to the full white paper. Post from Aaron's personal LinkedIn. ~750 words.*

---

**Headline:** Your school had a healthy year. So where did the money go?

---

A charter leader walked me through her benefits renewal a while back. Claims had been light all year. Nobody had a major health event, the staff stayed healthy, the numbers looked good from where she sat. Then the renewal landed with another increase on it, and she asked me the question I hear more than any other.

"If we barely used it, why does it keep going up?"

Here's the honest answer nobody had ever given her. On the plan she was on, a healthy year was never going to come back to her. She paid a fixed premium, the insurer carried the risk, and the insurer kept the surplus. That is how a fully insured plan is built. You can run a clean year, save the carrier real money, and watch every dollar of it stay with the carrier. She'd done everything right and the design of the plan meant she'd never see a cent of it.

What got to me is that her generalist broker had never told her there was another version. Not because it's exotic. Schools her size run on the alternatives right now. It's because renewing the same fully insured plan every year and passing along the increase is easy, and the alternatives ask the broker to actually do some work.

I grew up around manual labor and a ranch, where if you did a job halfway, something broke and you owned it. That's the standard I expected when I got into insurance. A lot of charter schools are not getting it, and benefits is where I see it most.

So here's the plain version of what she'd never been shown.

Health plan funding runs on a spectrum. Fully insured sits at one end: fixed premium, insurer keeps the upside. Full self-funding sits at the other: you pay claims directly and keep the savings, but on its own it carries real volatility, and a single catastrophic claim can blow a hole in a smaller employer's budget. Most individual schools don't have enough covered lives to make that math stable alone.

Between those two ends sit the models actually worth a charter school's attention.

A level-funded plan is the practical first step. You pay a set monthly amount, so you keep the budget predictability you're used to. That payment splits into three parts: a claims fund, the administrative fee, and the premium for stop-loss insurance. Stop-loss is the safety net. It caps your exposure, so if claims run high, the overage gets absorbed and your downside stays limited to what you already budgeted. And the part fully insured never gave her: if your group's claims come in below the funded amount, you get the surplus back as a refund. A healthy year finally pays you instead of the carrier. You also get real monthly claims data, so for the first time you can see what's driving your costs instead of being handed a number with no explanation.

A group captive is the next level of control. It's an insurance company owned by the employers in it. Like-sized employers pool together, self-fund their own routine claims, share a middle layer so one member's rough year is cushioned by the group, and put reinsurance on top for the truly catastrophic claims. Dollars not spent on claims come back to members as dividends instead of staying with an insurer. The charter angle is the one worth sitting with: one school may be too small to do this alone, but charter schools share a risk profile, and banding together reaches a scale none of them could reach solo.

Now the part any honest broker says out loud. These models move some risk onto the school. In a bad claims year you will pay more than your best case, up to the point where stop-loss or reinsurance takes over. That ceiling is defined and known in advance. The tradeoff is a capped downside in exchange for keeping the upside in a good year. Anyone who pitches this as free money is lying to you.

I wrote the full breakdown up on our site, no jargon, no gate, free to read and free to forward to your board or your business manager: **[LINK TO WHITE PAPER]**

If you do one thing with this, ask whoever handles your benefits one question.

Has anyone ever run your school's real claims history against a level-funded or captive model and shown you, in dollars, what a healthy year would have returned to your budget instead of the insurer's? If the answer is no, you've been renewing the most expensive version of this without ever seeing the alternative.

Aaron Schwen

---

checklist.label: Company-page checklist / matrix
checklist.content_markdown:
# Company Page Spoke: Health Plan Funding Model Comparison and Evaluation Checklist

*Spoke type: Company storefront page (authoritative, technical, credibility-building). Goal: validate Charter Select to a prospect actively vetting us. Post from the Charter Select company page. Lead with the matrix, then the checklist.*

---

**Headline:** Fully insured isn't your only option. Before you renew, see how the funding models actually compare, including the downside.

Health plan funding runs along a spectrum, and where a charter school sits on it decides whether a healthy year returns money to the budget or stays with the insurer. Here is how the four funding models compare on the points that matter to a school: who carries the risk, who keeps the surplus, the data you get back, and the honest downside. Below the matrix is the checklist we run before recommending any change.

## The funding spectrum, model by model

| Model | Who carries the risk | Surplus in a good year | Claims data you get | The honest downside |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fully insured** | Insurer carries all of it | Insurer keeps it | Little to none; you get a renewal number | None on claims, but you never share in a healthy year |
| **Level-funded** | Shared; stop-loss caps your exposure | Returned to the school as a refund | Real monthly claims data (ER usage, Rx, cost drivers) | Pay more than best case in a bad year, up to the stop-loss ceiling; more compliance; not sold in every state |
| **Group captive** | Pooled across member employers; reinsurance on top | Returned to members as dividends | Deep data; underwritten on your own performance | Variable cost in a bad year up to the reinsurance layer; requires scale and active management |
| **Full self-funding** | Employer carries it directly | Employer keeps all of it | Full data and control | Real volatility; a single catastrophic claim can blow a hole in a smaller budget |

The two middle models, level-funded and group captive, are the ones built for the small-to-midsize employer a charter school usually is. A single school may be too small to self-fund alone, but charter schools share a risk profile, which is exactly what makes captive pooling reach a scale none of them could reach solo.

## The funding evaluation checklist

- [ ] Pull your actual claims history, not a generic benchmark, before comparing anything.
- [ ] Confirm your state allows level-funded plans (stop-loss regulation varies by state).
- [ ] Model a level-funded plan against your real claims and quantify the refund in a healthy year.
- [ ] Read the stop-loss contract terms; the plan lives or dies on them.
- [ ] Confirm the stop-loss ceiling so you know your maximum exposure in a bad year before you sign.
- [ ] If size or appetite supports it, evaluate a group captive and its three layers (self-funded, shared, reinsurance).
- [ ] Confirm you will receive monthly claims data and that someone is committed to managing it.
- [ ] Document the modeled downside, not just the upside, for the board record.

## Why this is standard practice for us and rare elsewhere

A generalist broker defaults to fully insured because it requires nothing of them: renew the same plan, pass along the increase, move on. These alternatives demand real claims analysis, careful stop-loss contract review, and ongoing management. We run the funding analysis on your real claims data before recommending a single change, and we walk you through the downside, because deciding without the whole picture is how schools end up overpaying for the most expensive version of their plan.

**Read the full breakdown:** [LINK TO WHITE PAPER]

---

topics.title: Better Benefits For Your Teachers And A Smaller Bill, Built From Parts Your Broker Never Mentioned
topics.subtitle: The full state-of-the-art benefits stack, plus the member-support layer that makes it actually work, so your staff get better care while your plan spends less.

hub.content_markdown:
# Better Benefits For Your Teachers And A Smaller Bill, Built From Parts Your Broker Never Mentioned

*The full state-of-the-art benefits stack, plus the member-support layer that makes it actually work, so your staff get better care while your plan spends less.*

---

A charter school superintendent told me she'd been promised "the best plan on the market" for six years running. Same carrier, same bundle, premium up every spring. So I asked her four questions. Who negotiated your pharmacy contract. Who picked your network. Who watched the order of payment for the staff member who turned 65 and kept working. Who a teacher actually called the morning she was handed a cancer diagnosis.

She went quiet on all four. Nobody had built any of it. She'd been sold one product, six times, and told it was a strategy.

---

## A plan is not a strategy

Most charter schools are handed a finished thing. One carrier, one network, one pharmacy manager, one set of rules, all welded together and sold as a single package. It is easy to buy. It renews itself. And it quietly costs your school more every year while handing your staff care that is slower and shallower than it should be.

The schools that break out of that cycle stop buying a bundle and start assembling a stack. Every piece gets chosen on its own merits, priced on its own, and swapped out when a better piece exists. Then they do the part nearly everyone skips. They wrap the whole thing in a support layer, so a regular teacher can use it without a benefits degree. This is the blueprint, the full build, top to bottom.

## The base: own the risk, then pool it

The foundation is the funding model. A fully insured plan hands your premium to a carrier, who keeps whatever your staff don't spend. A self-funded or level-funded plan flips that. Your school pays for the actual claims your people generate, with stop-loss insurance standing behind you to cap the catastrophic ones. In a good year, the savings stay with your school instead of padding a carrier's margin. The step up from there is a captive: a group of self-funded employers who pool their stop-loss risk together so no one school eats a brutal claims year alone. You get the cost control of self-funding with the stability of a much larger group. The base layer is a level-funded or self-funded plan with a clear path toward a captive as you grow.

## Unbundle it, then rent what you need

This is where the bundle breaks open. In a self-funded plan, you do not have to buy the carrier's all-in-one package. You unbundle it. You hire each function separately and keep only the ones that serve you.

The administrator is the engine room. An independent Third Party Administrator, a TPA that is not owned by a carrier or a hospital system, processes your claims and runs your plan while answering to you instead of to a parent insurer. Boon-Chapman is a clean example of that category. It has administered self-funded plans since 1961, the oldest TPA in Texas, and it has stayed deliberately independent of carrier and hospital ownership, which is the whole point: it is structurally free to act in the plan's interest because no carrier signs its paycheck. That independence is what unbundling actually buys you. You keep control of plan design. You get real access to your own claims data instead of a carrier's filtered summary. And you can plug specialized vendors straight into the plan, because an independent TPA is built to integrate and exchange data with outside partners rather than defaulting to whatever the carrier ships in the box.

The network is the next piece, and you do not have to marry a carrier to get one. You rent a national network through your TPA, so your staff keep broad provider access and in-network discounts without surrendering control of everything else to the carrier that owns it. Pair that rented network with reference-based pricing and price-transparency tools, which set fair payment limits against a public benchmark like Medicare rates instead of accepting a hospital's mystery chargemaster. Published savings on these models run real, often in the range of fifteen to thirty percent against traditional PPO claims, though the exact number depends on the group.

## The pharmacy: hire a PBM that works for you

I covered the pharmacy games in depth in the advanced-employee-care paper, so I will keep this short. Your drug benefit runs through a Pharmacy Benefit Manager. Most traditional PBMs profit from spread pricing and from keeping manufacturer rebates, which means the middleman wins when your plan overpays. The fix is an independent, transparent, pass-through PBM that charges a disclosed fee, passes the real drug cost straight through, and returns the rebates to your plan. Carved out and chosen on its own, not bundled in, it is one of the fastest ways to stop the bleeding at the pharmacy counter.

## The plan design: pay for primary care first

Now the design layer, the part your staff feel every week. The traditional plan lets everyone wander into the most expensive specialist and the most expensive facility by default. A high-value, accountable-care design points the other way. It anchors each member to a coordinated primary-care team that quarterbacks their care.

Centivo is a good example of this model in action. It is a self-funded health plan built around a primary-care-based Accountable Care Organization, with its own virtual primary care practice that earned the National Committee for Quality Assurance's Patient-Centered Medical Home recognition. The mechanism is worth understanding, because it explains why the savings are real and not a slogan. Centivo makes primary care free at the point of use. Members pay $0 to see their primary care team, and the result is that they use it, reportedly around 40 percent more than members on a traditional plan. More primary care up front means problems get caught early, fewer unnecessary specialist referrals, fewer avoidable imaging studies, fewer ER trips. Layer that on lower unit costs negotiated directly with high-value health systems, and Centivo reports savings commonly in the range of twenty percent or more versus traditional carriers, with one employer-coalition partnership reporting 23 to 36 percent. The logic is the same one behind Direct Primary Care, which I covered in the advanced-employee-care paper. Fund the front door of medicine, and the expensive failures downstream stop happening.

That is the cost-containment stack assembled. Independent TPA, rented network, reference-based pricing, transparent PBM, primary-care-anchored design, all sitting on a self-funded base headed toward a captive. Each piece earns its place. None of it is reserved for giant employers.

## The part nobody builds: protecting the members and the fund

A stack like this gives you control. Control means you are now responsible for things a carrier used to quietly handle, and getting them wrong is expensive. Two member populations decide whether your fund stays healthy.

**Medicare-eligible active employees.** When a teacher or staff member is 65 or older and still actively working, federal Medicare Secondary Payer rules decide who pays first. If your school has 20 or more employees, your group health plan pays primary and Medicare pays secondary. You cannot take that worker's Medicare eligibility into account, cannot push them off your plan, and must offer them the same benefits under the same conditions as everyone else. If you have fewer than 20 employees, Medicare generally pays primary. On a self-funded plan, getting this backwards is not a paperwork slip. It means your fund is paying claims Medicare should have covered, or denying claims it owes, and both carry real penalties and recovery actions. Someone has to get the order right and keep it right as your headcount moves across that threshold.

**COBRA continuants.** When an employee leaves, federal COBRA rules let them stay on your plan, and you can charge up to 102 percent of the full cost of coverage, the employee and employer share combined plus a two percent administrative load. On a fully insured plan, that is the carrier's problem. On a self-funded plan, it is yours, and there is a sharp edge here. The people who choose to keep paying that full freight are disproportionately the ones with active, expensive medical needs. That is adverse selection, and it can load your fund with high-cost claimants who no longer even work for you. State "mini-COBRA" laws that soften this for small fully insured groups do not apply to a self-funded plan, so the exposure sits squarely on your fund. The protection is managing it deliberately: tracking continuants, pricing COBRA so the fund is not quietly subsidizing it, and structuring your stop-loss so a known high-cost claimant is handled correctly rather than draining the plan in silence. Stop-loss tools like individual lasers exist for exactly these situations, and they have to be set with eyes open at renewal.

This is the layer a generalist never touches, because a bundled carrier absorbed it invisibly and the broker never had to learn it. Once you own the risk, somebody who actually understands it has to stand guard over it.

## The high-touch layer: support people through the hardest moments

Cost control protects the fund. The next pieces protect the human being. These are the point solutions and advocacy services that turn a plan into actual care, and the unbundled TPA above is what lets you plug them in.

Specialized support for a serious diagnosis is the clearest example. Aaron points schools toward an oncology-support benefit, the kind he describes as a CancerCARE-style program. The mechanism is what makes the category worth evaluating. A credible example is AccessHope, built on partnerships with NCI-Designated Comprehensive Cancer Centers like City of Hope, Dana-Farber, and Fred Hutchinson. When a staff member hears the word cancer, a program like this connects that member's own treating oncologist with sub-specialist expertise from one of those centers, remotely, so the patient does not have to relocate to get a top-tier read on the case. Why this matters for both the member and the fund shows up in AccessHope's own reported numbers: their experts recommended improvements to the treatment plan in more than 90 percent of cases reviewed, and those recommendations were adopted into care about 75 percent of the time. Serious diagnoses drive a large share of any plan's spend, so getting the diagnosis and the treatment right at the start protects the member's life and the fund's balance in the same motion.

Care navigation and advocacy is the everyday version of the same idea. Aaron describes this as a CareGuide-style layer. The category, exemplified by firms like Included Health and Health Advocate, gives every member one number to call and a real person on the other end. That person becomes the single point of entry for the whole confusing system. They steer members toward genuinely high-quality providers, the kind of steerage that matters because prices for the same procedure in the same town can differ by as much as 400 percent. They redirect care to the right site, untangle a baffling bill, and answer the questions that otherwise end in a panicked, overpriced ER visit. Steer one imaging study or outpatient procedure to the right facility and you can save hundreds to a couple thousand dollars on that single claim, and your member gets a guide instead of a maze.

## The delivery layer: none of it matters if nobody uses it

You can build the best stack in the country and waste it if your staff never understand what they have. I went deep on this in the open-enrollment-modernization paper, so here is the short version. Educate at open enrollment, in formats people actually open, well before the deadline. Run one-on-one meetings so a teacher with a new baby and a teacher nearing retirement each pick the plan that fits their real life. And put the consumerism tools directly in members' hands, the navigation number, the transparency tool, the decision support, so that in the moment they need care, they are pointed toward the high-value provider and the right site without having to figure it out alone. A benefit nobody understands is money you spent for resentment.

## Why your school doesn't have this

Every piece I just described is available to a school your size right now. Independent TPAs, rented networks, transparent PBMs, primary-care-anchored designs, oncology and navigation point solutions, real enrollment support. None of it requires being a Fortune 500 company.

So why doesn't your school have it? Because assembling a stack and standing up a support layer is a different job than selling a renewal. A generalist broker who carries restaurants and law firms and your school in the same book shows up once a year with the same bundle and a higher premium, walks you through the increase, and calls it service. That broker will never carve out a PBM, vet an independent TPA, rent and pair a network with reference-based pricing, anchor the design on primary care, set up the Medicare order of payment, manage COBRA adverse selection against your stop-loss, or wire in a cancer-support program. The job they signed up for is pressing the easy button before the deadline. Building this is slow, technical, ongoing work. It only happens when someone treats your school as a real client instead of a file to clear off the desk.

## Before your next renewal

You do not need to memorize any of this. You need to look honestly at one thing.

When you think about the plan your staff have right now, can you name who built each piece of it, who is watching the Medicare order and the COBRA exposure so your fund is not quietly paying claims it should not, and who a teacher actually calls the day she is handed a serious diagnosis? Or did the whole thing just renew itself again while everyone was busy running a school?

If you cannot answer that with confidence, you do not have a benefits problem yet. You have a stack nobody assembled and a support layer nobody built. Both can be built. The only question is whether anyone on your account ever intended to.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want someone to assemble the whole stack and stand up the member-support layer behind it, the funding base, the independent TPA, the transparent pharmacy, the right plan design, the Medicare and COBRA guardrails, and the navigation your teachers can actually call, that is the build we do before anyone talks about switching a plan.*

# LinkedIn Personal Newsletter: The Benefits Stack Nobody Assembled

*Spoke type: Personal profile newsletter (Aaron's voice). Goal: human story + compressed insight, driving to the full white paper. Post from Aaron's personal LinkedIn. ~750 words.*

---

**Headline:** I asked a superintendent four questions about her health plan. She went quiet on all four. That silence cost her staff and her budget for six straight years.

---

A charter school superintendent told me she had the best plan on the market. She knew it because her broker had told her so, every spring, for six years running. Same carrier. Same bundle. Premium up a little each time, but that was just the market, she'd been told.

So I asked her four questions. Who negotiated your pharmacy contract. Who picked your network. Who is watching the order of payment for the staff member who turned 65 and kept teaching. Who does a teacher actually call the morning she's handed a cancer diagnosis.

She went quiet on all four. Not because she didn't care. Because nobody had ever built any of it. She'd been sold one product, six times, and told it was a strategy.

That conversation is most of why I do this work the way I do.

Here's the mechanic, in plain English. Most charter schools get handed a finished thing. One carrier, one network, one pharmacy manager, one set of rules, all welded together and sold as a single package. It's easy to buy. It renews itself. And it quietly costs the school more every year while handing staff care that's slower and shallower than it should be.

The schools that break that cycle stop buying a bundle and start assembling a stack. Every piece gets chosen on its own, priced on its own, swapped out when something better shows up. The funding base flips from fully insured to self-funded or level-funded, so a good claims year saves the school money instead of padding a carrier's margin. The pharmacy benefit gets carved out to a transparent, pass-through manager that returns the rebates instead of pocketing them. The plan design anchors on free primary care, because funding the front door of medicine stops the expensive failures downstream. An independent administrator runs the whole thing answering to the school, not to a parent carrier.

That's the cost-control half. It's real, and it's available to a school your size right now.

But here's where almost every build I see falls apart. Once you own the risk, you own things a carrier used to handle quietly, and getting them wrong is expensive. The teacher who's 65 and still working triggers federal Medicare rules about who pays first. Get the order backwards on a self-funded plan and your fund pays claims Medicare should have covered, with real penalties behind it. The employee who leaves and stays on COBRA can load your fund with high-cost claims from someone who no longer works for you. Somebody has to stand guard over both, deliberately, at every renewal.

And then there's the part that's actually about the human being. When a staff member hears the word cancer, does a real person connect her treating oncologist with sub-specialist expertise from a top cancer center, so she gets the right read without uprooting her life? When a teacher gets a baffling bill or a scary referral, is there one number to call and a real guide on the other end, steering her to a high-quality provider and the right site of care? Prices for the same procedure in the same town can differ by 400 percent. A guide saves the member money and saves the fund money in the same motion.

You can build the best stack in the country and waste it if nobody on staff understands what they have. A benefit nobody uses is money you spent for resentment.

So why doesn't your school have all of this? Because assembling a stack and standing up a support layer is a different job than selling a renewal. A generalist who carries restaurants and law firms and your school in the same book shows up once a year with the same bundle and a higher premium, walks you through the increase, and calls it service. Building the real thing is slow, technical, ongoing work. It only happens when someone treats your school as a real client instead of a file to clear off the desk.

I wrote the full build up on our site, top to bottom, no jargon, no gate, free to read and free to forward to your board or business manager: **[LINK TO WHITE PAPER]**

If you do one thing with this, do this. Picture the plan your staff have right now, and ask yourself: can you name who built each piece of it, who's watching the Medicare order and the COBRA exposure, and who a teacher actually calls the day she's handed a serious diagnosis? Or did the whole thing just renew itself again while everyone was busy running a school?

If you can't answer that with confidence, you don't have a benefits problem yet. You have a stack nobody assembled.

Aaron Schwen

---

checklist.content_markdown:
# Company Page Spoke: The Full Charter School Benefits Stack and Member-Support Layer

*Spoke type: Company storefront page (authoritative, technical, credibility-building). Goal: validate Charter Select to a prospect actively vetting us. Post from the Charter Select company page. Lead with the matrix, then the checklist.*

---

**Headline:** A health plan is not a strategy. Here is the full stack we assemble for a charter school, layer by layer, plus the support layer that makes it actually work.

Most charter schools are handed one carrier, one network, one pharmacy manager, all welded together and sold as a single package. The schools that spend less and care better stop buying that bundle and start assembling a stack, where every piece is chosen on its own merits, then wrapped in a support layer a regular teacher can use without a benefits degree. Here is the full build and the readiness checklist we run before anyone talks about switching a plan.

## The stack, layer by layer

| Layer | What it does | The piece we assemble | Why it beats the bundle |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Funding base** | Decides who keeps the savings in a good claims year | Level-funded or self-funded plan with stop-loss, on a path toward a captive | Savings stay with the school instead of padding a carrier's margin; a captive pools risk so no single school eats a brutal year alone |
| **Independent PBM** | Runs the drug benefit | Transparent, pass-through PBM, carved out and chosen on its own | Disclosed fee, real drug cost passed through, rebates returned to your plan, instead of spread pricing and retained rebates |
| **RBP and price transparency** | Sets fair payment limits on facility and provider bills | Reference-based pricing benchmarked to a public standard, with transparency tools | Caps payment against a public benchmark instead of a hospital's mystery chargemaster; published savings often run 15 to 30 percent versus traditional PPO claims |
| **Unbundled TPA + rented network** | Processes claims, runs the plan, provides provider access | Independent TPA (carrier- and hospital-independent) plus a rented national network | The administrator answers to the plan, not a parent insurer; you keep plan-design control, real claims data, and the ability to plug in outside vendors |
| **Accountable-care design** | Points members toward the right care by default | Primary-care-anchored design with free, coordinated primary care | Free front-door primary care gets used more, catching problems early; reported savings commonly 20 percent or more versus traditional carriers |
| **MSP and COBRA guardrails** | Protects the fund from rules a carrier used to absorb | Active Medicare Secondary Payer order-of-payment management and deliberate COBRA pricing against stop-loss | On a self-funded plan, a wrong Medicare order or unmanaged COBRA adverse selection drains the fund and carries penalties; somebody has to stand guard |
| **Member support and navigation** | Protects the human being in the hardest moments | Oncology-support program plus care navigation and advocacy | A real person and sub-specialist cancer expertise on call; steerage matters when the same procedure can vary by up to 400 percent in price in one town |

## The assembly and readiness checklist

- [ ] Confirm the funding base: level-funded or self-funded with appropriate stop-loss, and map the path to a captive as headcount grows.
- [ ] Carve out the pharmacy benefit to a transparent, pass-through PBM with disclosed fees and rebates returned to the plan.
- [ ] Pair a rented national network with reference-based pricing and live price-transparency tools.
- [ ] Place administration with a carrier- and hospital-independent TPA; confirm direct access to your own claims data.
- [ ] Anchor the plan design on coordinated primary care; confirm low or zero member cost at the point of primary care.
- [ ] Set the Medicare Secondary Payer order of payment correctly for active employees 65+, and re-verify it as headcount crosses the 20-employee threshold.
- [ ] Track COBRA continuants, price COBRA so the fund is not silently subsidizing it, and structure stop-loss (including lasers where appropriate) for known high-cost claimants at renewal.
- [ ] Stand up the member-support layer: an oncology-support benefit and a care-navigation and advocacy line, plugged into the TPA.
- [ ] Put the consumerism tools directly in members' hands: the navigation number, the transparency tool, and decision support, delivered at open enrollment in formats people actually open.

## Why this is standard practice for us

A generalist broker who carries restaurants, law firms, and your school in the same book shows up once a year with the same bundle and a higher premium, and calls it service. That broker will not carve out a PBM, vet an independent TPA, pair a rented network with reference-based pricing, anchor the design on primary care, set the Medicare order of payment, manage COBRA adverse selection against stop-loss, or wire in a cancer-support program. We do, because we work with charter schools exclusively and treat the build as the actual job, not the renewal as the easy button. Every piece above is available to a school your size right now. The only question is whether anyone on your account ever intended to assemble it.

**Read the full breakdown:** [LINK TO WHITE PAPER]

---

*Posting notes: this is the credibility asset. Keep it clean and technical. The matrix and checklist also work as a downloadable PDF lead-capture on the website (top/bottom of the hub page). Replace [LINK TO WHITE PAPER] with the live URL.*

topics.title: Your School Had a Healthy Year. Your Reward Was a 12% Premium Increase. Here's Where the Money Went.
topics.subtitle: Why the standard health plan most charter schools carry is built so the school loses in a good year and loses in a bad one, and what that costs a budget that can't absorb it.

hub.content_markdown:
# Your School Had a Healthy Year. Your Reward Was a 12% Premium Increase. Here's Where the Money Went.

*Why the standard health plan most charter schools carry is built so the school loses in a good year and loses in a bad one, and what that costs a budget that can't absorb it.*

---

Your staff had a healthy year. No major surgeries, no catastrophic claims, a quiet twelve months. By any honest measure, your health plan made money.

Then renewal arrives, and the premium goes up double digits anyway.

So where did the money from your healthy year go? It went to the insurer, and you are never getting it back. That's not a glitch. That's how the plan was designed to work, and most school leaders have never had it explained to them.

---

## How a fully insured plan actually treats you

The plan most charter schools carry is called fully insured. You pay a fixed premium every month. The insurance company takes on the risk of paying claims. Simple, predictable, and on the surface it sounds fair.

Here's the part that isn't fair, and it comes down to one question: who keeps the money in a good year?

In a fully insured plan, if your people stay healthy and claims come in well below what you paid in premium, the insurer keeps the difference. All of it. The surplus your healthy workforce generated becomes the insurer's profit. You don't get a refund. You don't get a credit. You often don't even get told it happened.

Now flip it. If you have a bad year and claims run high, the insurer covers it, then comes back at renewal and raises your premium to recover the cost and protect their margin.

Read those two outcomes together and you'll see the trap. Good year, they keep the surplus. Bad year, they raise your rate. You carry the downside in both directions and capture the upside in neither. Heads they win, tails you lose.

## You're also managing the cost completely blind

There's a quieter problem stacked on top of the financial one. A fully insured plan gives you almost no usable claims data.

You can't see what's driving your costs. You don't know if it's a handful of large claims, a pharmacy spend problem, overuse of the emergency room, or something you could actually address. You're handed a renewal number and told to take it or leave it, with no window into how that number was built.

You can't manage what you can't see. Fully insured keeps you from seeing it, which conveniently keeps you dependent on the renewal the insurer hands you.

## Why this lands so hard on a school

For a school, every premium dollar is a dollar with somewhere better to be. It's a classroom aide, a counselor, a raise that keeps a good teacher from leaving for the district down the road. A double-digit increase isn't a line item you shrug off. It's a real cut somewhere else in the building.

And recruitment makes it worse. You're competing for staff against public districts that often have deep benefits and pension systems behind them. When your health plan eats your budget and still raises rates every year, you lose ground on the exact tool you'd use to attract and keep good people. The fully insured model quietly works against the thing your school most needs to do.

## There is another way the math can run

Here's what your generalist broker probably never told you: the surplus from a healthy year does not have to disappear into the insurer's pocket. There are funding models built so that a good claims year puts money back into your budget instead of theirs, and they are not reserved for giant corporations. Schools your size use them.

Those models come with their own structure and their own honest tradeoffs, which is its own conversation and its own paper. The point here is narrower and it's this: if your school is fully insured, you are in the one arrangement where you are guaranteed to never benefit from your own people's good health. You should at least know that's the deal you're in.

## What a broker who's actually working would have shown you

A broker doing the job walks you through where your premium dollars went, tells you plainly whether you had a good or bad claims year, and lays the alternative funding models on the table so you can decide with real numbers. They treat your health spend as something you can manage, not a fixed cost you're stuck absorbing.

A generalist renews you fully insured every single year because it's the easy button. No data to pull, no analysis to run, no alternatives to explain. They re-up the same plan, pass along the increase, and call it service.

## Before you accept your next renewal

You don't need a benefits degree to find out where you stand. You need to ask one question and watch how fast you get a straight answer.

So here's the one to ask:

The last time your staff had a healthy claims year, what happened to the money? Did any of it come back to your school, or did the insurer keep all of it and raise your rate anyway? If your broker can't answer that clearly, you've just learned something about both your plan and your broker.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want to see where your premium dollars actually go and what your alternatives look like in real numbers, that's the analysis we run before we ever talk about switching anything.*

---

**Headline:** Your school had a healthy year. Your reward was a 12% premium hike. Here's where that money went.

Not a refund, not a credit, not even a heads up that it happened. That wasn't a glitch in her plan. That was her plan working exactly the way it was built to work.

I grew up around manual labor and a ranch. If you did a job halfway out there, somebody got hurt or something broke, and you owned it. That's the standard I expected when I got into insurance. It is not the standard most charter schools are getting, and the fully insured health plan is one of the clearest examples I know.

Read those two outcomes together and you'll see the trap. Good year, they keep the surplus. Bad year, they raise your rate. You carry the downside in both directions and capture the upside in neither.

There's a quieter problem stacked on top of that one. A fully insured plan hands you almost no usable claims data. You can't see whether your costs are being driven by a few large claims, a pharmacy spend problem, or overuse of the emergency room. You can't manage what you can't see, and staying blind keeps you dependent on whatever number the insurer hands you.

For a school this lands harder than almost anywhere. Every premium dollar is a dollar with somewhere better to be. A classroom aide. A counselor. A raise that keeps a good teacher from leaving for the district down the road. And you're already competing for staff against public districts with deep benefits and pension systems behind them. Those models carry their own structure and their own honest tradeoffs, and that's a separate paper. The point here is narrower. If your school is fully insured, you are in the one arrangement where you are guaranteed to never benefit from your own people's good health. You should at least know that's the deal you're in.

A broker doing the actual job walks you through where your premium dollars went, tells you plainly whether you had a good or bad claims year, and lays the alternatives on the table with real numbers. A generalist renews you fully insured every year because it's the easy button. No data to pull, no analysis to run. They re-up the same plan, pass along the increase, and call it service. That's the part I can't stomach, and it's most of why Charter Select exists and works only with charter schools.

I wrote the full breakdown up on our site. No jargon, no gate, free to read and free to forward to your board or your business manager: **[LINK TO WHITE PAPER]**

If you only do one thing with this, ask whoever handles your benefits one question.

The last time your staff had a healthy claims year, what happened to the money? Did any of it come back to your school, or did the insurer keep all of it and raise your rate anyway?

If they can't answer that clearly, you just learned something about both your plan and your broker.

Forward this to the school leader who needs it. This one's too expensive to keep guessing at.

Aaron Schwen

checklist.label: Company-page checklist / matrix
checklist.content_markdown:
# Company Page Spoke: Fully Insured vs. The Alternative, A Funding Model Comparison and Renewal Checklist

*Spoke type: Company storefront page (authoritative, technical, credibility-building). Goal: validate Charter Select to a prospect actively vetting us. Post from the Charter Select company page. Lead with the matrix, then the checklist.*

---

**Headline:** Before you accept another fully insured renewal, understand what the model does with your money in a good year and a bad one.

The plan most charter schools carry is fully insured: a fixed monthly premium, with the insurer taking on all claims risk. It looks simple and predictable. The structural problem is who keeps the surplus when your staff stays healthy, and who absorbs the increase when they don't. Here is how the fully insured model treats your money against the alternative funding approach the model leaves out, and the checklist we run before any school accepts a renewal.

## How the funding model treats your money

| Scenario | Fully insured | Alternative funding |
|---|---|---|
| **Good claims year (low claims)** | Insurer keeps the surplus. No refund, no credit, no notice. | Surplus can come back into your budget instead of the insurer's. |
| **Bad claims year (high claims)** | Insurer covers claims, then raises your rate at renewal to recover and protect margin. | Defined structure for high-claim years, reviewed with real numbers and honest tradeoffs. |
| **Claims data visibility** | Almost none. You get a renewal number with no window into how it was built. | Usable claims data, so you can see what is actually driving cost. |
| **Cost management** | Treated as a fixed cost you absorb. | Treated as a spend you can analyze and manage. |
| **Who carries the risk position** | You carry the downside in both directions, capture the upside in neither. | Designed so a healthy workforce can work in your favor. |

The fully insured arrangement is the one where a school is guaranteed to never benefit from its own people's good health. Alternative funding models are not reserved for large corporations; schools of this size use them. They carry their own structure and tradeoffs, which is a separate analysis, not a default switch.

## The renewal review checklist

- [ ] Confirm your current funding model in writing (fully insured, level-funded, or other).
- [ ] Ask where the surplus went the last time your staff had a healthy claims year.
- [ ] Request your claims experience for the plan year, not just the renewal number.
- [ ] Identify the top cost drivers (large claims, pharmacy spend, emergency room overuse) before accepting any increase.
- [ ] Have the renewal increase explained against actual claims, not "the market."
- [ ] Get the alternative funding models laid out with real numbers for your group, including their honest tradeoffs.
- [ ] Compare what the program costs you against what those dollars do elsewhere in the building (staff, counselors, retention).
- [ ] Document the funding decision and the rationale for the board record.

## Why this is standard practice for us and rare elsewhere

A generalist broker renews a school fully insured every year because it is the easy button. No data to pull, no analysis to run, no alternatives to explain. They re-up the same plan, pass along the increase, and call it service. We treat your health spend as something you can manage, walk you through where your premium dollars went, and put the alternatives on the table with real numbers, because for a school every premium dollar is a dollar with somewhere better to be.

**Read the full breakdown:** [LINK TO WHITE PAPER]

---

Your staff had a healthy year. No big claims, no surgeries, twelve quiet months.

In your health plan, who keeps the money when your people stay healthy?
Not your school. The insurer keeps every dollar of the surplus.

Your fully insured plan hands you a renewal number and tells you to take it or leave it.

Every premium dollar your health plan eats is a classroom aide you didn't hire.

You're competing for staff against districts with deep benefits and pensions behind them.

Your generalist broker probably never told you this:
the surplus from a healthy year does not have to vanish into the insurer's pocket.

One question for whoever handles your school's benefits:

topics.title: Two Plans Both Called Level Funded, And Only One Lets You See Where Your Money Went
topics.subtitle: A plain-English comparison of independent level-funded programs and the big carriers' level-funded products: same label, very different machinery, and why the broker only ever shows you one of them.

hub.content_markdown:
# Two Plans Both Called Level Funded, And Only One Lets You See Where Your Money Went

*A plain-English comparison of independent level-funded programs and the big carriers' level-funded products: same label, very different machinery, and why the broker only ever shows you one of them.*

---

Your broker says he found you a level-funded plan. Good. You should be glad he did.

Then ask him a second question. Which one? Because the words on the brochure can read identical while the engine underneath runs on opposite logic. One version is engineered so your nurse uses the plan more and pays less at the counter, hands you your claims data, and returns every unused dollar. The other bundles all of that inside the carrier and sends you a number once a year with no map of how it got there.

Most charter leaders only ever get shown the second one. Below is why, and the difference it makes.

---

## A quick reset on what level funding even is

The full story lives in our piece on alternative funding, so here is the short one. A level-funded plan lets a smaller employer self-fund with training wheels. You pay a steady monthly amount, split three ways: a claims fund to pay medical bills, an administrative fee, and the stop-loss premium that caps your downside if claims run hot. In a healthy year, the money you funded but did not spend can come back to you, instead of the carrier pocketing your good year by default.

That structure is the same no matter who builds it. The fight is over who builds it, and what they let you keep, see, and control while they do.

## Same label, two different machines

When a generalist broker says level-funded, he usually means a packaged product from one of the big medical carriers. Cigna, UnitedHealthcare, and the Blues all sell one. They are real plans, they are ACA-compliant, and under federal law they sit on the self-funded side with the employer carrying claims risk inside the stop-loss limits. Cigna, for example, rolls claims funding, the stop-loss premium, and admin fees into one preset monthly payment, and if claims run high you do not pay more that year. UnitedHealthcare markets its level-funded product as costing employers meaningfully less on average than a comparable fully insured plan. That is real, and it is fine work.

The other machine is an independent level-funded program. Two names worth knowing here are Gravie and Benecon. Same three-part funding structure, same stop-loss safety net, but the program is assembled from parts the employer can actually inspect and swap, and built around a different idea of who the plan is supposed to serve.

The distinction shows up in four specific places, and the independents each made a deliberate bet in one of them.

## One: the plan can be designed for the people using it, not just the employer buying it

A carrier level-funded plan is mostly a funding wrapper around a standard copay or HSA design. The shape of the benefit is the same thing your staff has always had: a deductible, copays, coinsurance, an out-of-pocket maximum. The savings story is aimed at the employer's renewal, not at the teacher standing at the pharmacy counter.

Gravie built its flagship plan, Comfort, on the opposite premise. It eliminates deductibles and copays on roughly 85 percent of the most common care: office visits, specialist visits, labs and imaging, generic prescriptions, urgent care, mental health, virtual care. Those carry zero out-of-pocket cost, paid first dollar. The tradeoff is honest and worth stating plainly: less common, expensive events like surgery or a hospital stay can cost the member more than they would on a traditional plan, up to an out-of-pocket maximum the employee chooses from a few options, after which the plan covers everything. The bet is that most people, most years, use the common stuff, so removing the price tag on routine care gets people to use it before a small problem becomes an expensive one. Gravie reported that for 2022, employers averaged a 15 percent premium reduction against their prior carrier renewal, and employees on Comfort spent meaningfully less out of pocket than peers on traditional copay or HSA plans.

That is the founder's read, not a slogan: a plan your staff will actually use early is a plan that bends the claims curve, and a plan that bends the claims curve is the one that keeps returning surplus.

## Two: who can see the claims data, and whether you can swap the pharmacy

Both kinds of plan are technically self-funded, so the employer is supposed to own the claims data either way. The difference is how usable it is when it reaches you.

A carrier-administered plan tends to hand you summary reporting formatted the way the carrier prefers. The pharmacy spend in particular often sits carved in, meaning drug pricing, rebates, and utilization stay folded inside the medical plan's reporting. One vendor, one bill, looks clean. The cost of that clean look is that you cannot easily see what your drugs cost or where the rebate dollars went, and the carrier's own pharmacy benefit manager is already bundled in. UnitedHealthcare's Optum Rx and Cigna's Express Scripts both arrive attached to their respective level-funded products.

Independent programs are built to be unbundled. You can read the cost drivers month over month, and you can carve the pharmacy out and shop it. Pass-through pharmacy pricing, where the PBM charges a clear fee and passes rebates back instead of hiding margin in the spread, used to be the independent advantage alone. The big PBMs are now being pushed the same direction: Optum Rx is shifting to clearly defined per-member fees, and Express Scripts is moving network reimbursement toward a cost-plus model in 2026. Gravie itself moved its PBM to Express Scripts effective January 1, 2026, replacing CVS Caremark. Forget which PBM has the cleanest contract this quarter. What matters is that an independent structure lets you re-shop the pharmacy when the market shifts, while a sealed carrier product makes you wait for the carrier to decide to shift it for you.

## Three: how much of a healthy year you actually get back

Surplus return is where the brochures blur together and the contracts do not.

Benecon's model is the clean illustration. Through its VERIS consortium, Benecon runs as an independent administrator, and the design is pay for what you use: each group keeps its own claim-fund account, risk is not commingled, no group subsidizes another, and the groups pool only to buy stop-loss together for better pricing. What you do not spend on claims, you keep, with Benecon advertising 100 percent surplus return alongside guaranteed renewals and rate caps. A healthy year is fully yours, and it is written that way on purpose.

Carrier level-funded products do return surplus too, but the terms vary and the fine print earns its keep. Cigna's level-funded settlement, as one published example, returns a share of the savings, often half or more, frequently as an administrative-fee credit rather than cash, and some carrier programs require you to renew to collect it at all. One wrinkle applies to everyone, independent or carrier: if employee contributions funded part of the premium, ERISA may treat a slice of the surplus as plan assets that must flow back to participants rather than to the school's general budget. A program built around surplus return handles that cleanly. A product where surplus is an afterthought handles it quietly.

## Four: advocacy and lock-in

Carrier plans route your staff to a general call center and tie you to that carrier's network and PBM economics, so leaving means rebuilding the whole arrangement somewhere else.

The independents tend to treat member help as part of the product. Gravie Care staffs licensed advocates who help members pick a plan, understand a bill, and sort out a claim year-round, and Benecon's ConnectCare3 arm runs nurse navigation and chronic-care support. For a charter staff with no HR department to absorb benefits questions, that hand-holding has real weight. And because an independent program is assembled from interchangeable parts, a network or administrator change does not force you to tear the whole plan down.

## The fair part, because pretending there are no tradeoffs is how brokers lie

The big carriers earn their scale honestly in two areas, and a school should weigh them.

Network breadth is the first. The national carriers have the widest provider networks in the country, and for a school with staff spread across a region, that reach has real value. An independent program has to prove its network covers your people before you trust it, and Comfort's richest savings show up only when staff actually use in-network common care.

Simplicity is the second. One carrier, one bill, one phone number is genuinely easier to administer than a program with separately chosen parts. That ease is worth something to a small school office, and it is the honest appeal of the packaged product.

So the verdict here is a point of view, not a law of physics. In Aaron's experience placing these plans, the independents take the cake for a charter school often enough that they should never be left off the table. A plan designed so people use routine care early, claims data you can actually read, a pharmacy you can re-shop, surplus terms that say 100 percent in writing, and advocates who pick up the phone tend to outrun the carrier's network-and-simplicity edge once you run the real numbers. Reasonable people can land differently on a given case. The real problem is that most schools never get to choose at all.

## Why you only ever saw one of them

A generalist broker sells the carrier's level-funded product for the same reason water runs downhill. It is the easy one. The carrier has a quoting portal he already knows, a sales rep who feeds him the proposal, and a familiar name the board will recognize. He clicks a few buttons and looks like he did something modern.

Showing you an independent program is work. He would have to know the administrators, understand consumer-first plan design, read a stop-loss contract he did not get handed, and compare pharmacy and surplus terms line by line. That is hours he does not bill on an account he already thinks is too small. So he shows you the carrier box, calls it level funding, and lets you assume that is the whole category.

It is not the whole category. It is the easy corner of it.

## Before you sign whatever he put in front of you

You do not have to become a benefits expert to protect yourself here. You just have to ask the one question that reveals whether you were shown the market or shown the shortcut.

So sit with this. When your broker brought you a level-funded plan, did he also bring an independent program to compare it against, show you a design built around what your staff actually uses, and put in writing who controls the pharmacy and the data and how much of a healthy year comes back to your budget? Or did one carrier's product show up alone, dressed as the entire option set?

If only one machine ever crossed your desk, you were not given a choice. You were given a default. And a default is a fine thing to accept on purpose and an expensive thing to accept by accident.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. When we look at level funding, we put the independent programs and the carrier products on the same table, with the plan design, the data access, the pharmacy economics, and the surplus terms spelled out, so you are choosing the structure instead of inheriting the one that was easiest to quote.*

newsletter.label: LinkedIn personal newsletter
newsletter.content_markdown:
# LinkedIn Personal Newsletter: Two Plans, Both Called Level Funded

*Spoke type: Personal profile newsletter (Aaron's voice). Goal: human story + compressed insight, driving to the full white paper. Post from Aaron's personal LinkedIn. ~750 words.*

---

**Headline:** Two plans on my desk. Same name on both brochures. One handed the school its money back. The other sent a number once a year and called it a day.

---

A charter leader called me last spring, pretty pleased with herself. Her broker had brought her a level-funded plan. She'd heard the term, knew it was supposed to be smarter than the old fully insured setup, and she signed. Good instinct. I told her so.

Then I asked her the second question. Which level-funded plan? She went quiet. There was only one on the table.

That's the part nobody warns school leaders about. "Level funded" is a label, not a machine. Two plans can carry that exact phrase on the cover and run on opposite logic underneath. One is engineered so your teacher pays nothing at the pharmacy counter for the routine stuff, hands you your claims data in a form you can actually read, lets you re-shop the pharmacy when the market moves, and returns every unused dollar from a healthy year. The other folds all of that inside one big carrier and mails you a settlement number twelve months later with no map of how it got there.

Most charter leaders only ever get shown the second one.

Here's the mechanic in plain English. Every level-funded plan splits your monthly payment three ways: a claims fund, an admin fee, and stop-loss premium that caps your downside if claims run hot. That structure is identical no matter who builds it. The fight is over who builds it and what they let you keep, see, and control while they do.

When a generalist broker says level funded, he almost always means a packaged product from one of the big medical carriers. Real plans, ACA-compliant, fine work. But they're a funding wrapper around a standard deductible-and-copay design, the pharmacy benefit manager comes welded in, the claims reporting arrives formatted the carrier's way, and the surplus terms often pay you back as an admin credit you only collect if you renew.

The other machine is an independent program. Gravie and Benecon are two names worth knowing. Same three-part funding, same stop-loss net, but assembled from parts you can inspect and swap. Gravie's Comfort plan throws out deductibles and copays on roughly 85 percent of common care, office visits, labs, generics, mental health, urgent care, paid first dollar, so your staff uses the plan early instead of waiting until a small problem turns expensive. Benecon's VERIS consortium keeps each group's claim fund separate, no commingling, and advertises 100 percent surplus return in writing. A healthy year is fully yours, on purpose.

I'll be fair, because pretending there are no tradeoffs is how brokers lie. The big carriers earn their scale honestly. They have the widest networks in the country, and one bill with one phone number is genuinely simpler for a small school office. Those things are worth something. But in my experience placing these plans, the independents take the cake for a charter school often enough that they should never be left off the table.

So why did that leader only see one? Because the carrier box is the easy one. The broker has a quoting portal he already knows and a rep who feeds him the proposal. Showing an independent program is hours of work, reading stop-loss contracts and comparing pharmacy terms line by line, on an account he already thinks is too small. So he shows the box, calls it level funding, and lets the school assume that's the whole category.

It isn't. It's the easy corner of it.

I grew up around manual labor and a ranch, where halfway work got somebody hurt. That's the standard I expected when I got into insurance, and it's why Charter Select puts the independent programs and the carrier products on the same table, with the plan design, the data access, the pharmacy economics, and the surplus terms spelled out.

I wrote the full breakdown on our site. No jargon, no gate, free to read and free to forward to your board or your business manager: **[LINK TO WHITE PAPER]**

If you do one thing with this, do this. Go back to the level-funded plan your broker brought you and ask whether he also put an independent program next to it, showed you a design built around what your staff actually uses, and put in writing who controls the pharmacy and the data and how much of a good year comes back to your budget. Or did one carrier's product show up alone, dressed as the entire option set?

If only one machine ever crossed your desk, you weren't given a choice. You were handed a default.

Aaron Schwen

---

checklist.label: Company-page checklist / matrix
checklist.content_markdown:
# Company Page Spoke: Independent vs. Carrier Level-Funded Comparison and Checklist

*Spoke type: Company storefront page (authoritative, technical, credibility-building). Goal: validate Charter Select to a prospect actively vetting us. Post from the Charter Select company page. Lead with the matrix, then the checklist.*

---

**Headline:** "Level funded" is a label, not a single product. Before you sign one, see what the independent programs do that the packaged carrier products do not.

Two plans can carry the identical phrase on the brochure and run on opposite machinery. Both split your monthly payment three ways (claims fund, admin fee, stop-loss premium) and both sit on the self-funded side of federal law. The difference is what the plan lets you keep, see, and control. Here is the side-by-side on the four places that decision actually gets made, followed by the checklist we run before we put any level-funded plan in front of a charter school.

## Independent level-funded vs. big-carrier level-funded

| Decision point | Independent program (e.g. Gravie, Benecon) | Big-carrier product (e.g. UHC, Cigna, Blues) |
|---|---|---|
| **Claims data access** | Itemized reporting you can read month over month; you own and can act on the cost drivers | Summary reporting formatted the carrier's way; pharmacy spend often carved in and hard to isolate |
| **PBM flexibility** | Unbundled; pharmacy can be carved out and re-shopped on pass-through pricing when the market shifts | Carrier's PBM bundled in by default (Optum Rx, Express Scripts); you wait for the carrier to change it |
| **Surplus return** | Designed around it; Benecon's VERIS advertises 100% surplus return in writing, with separate per-group claim accounts and no commingling | Returned too, but terms vary; often a share of savings paid as an admin-fee credit, and some require renewal to collect |
| **Portability / lock-in** | Built from interchangeable parts; a network or administrator change does not force a full teardown | Tied to that carrier's network and PBM economics; leaving means rebuilding the whole arrangement |

The honest counterweight: national carriers hold the widest provider networks in the country, and one carrier, one bill, one phone number is genuinely simpler to administer. Those advantages are real and a school should weigh them. The point of the matrix is that you weigh them against something, instead of inheriting the product that was easiest to quote.

## The level-funded evaluation checklist

- [ ] Confirm whether more than one level-funded structure was quoted, at least one independent program alongside any carrier product.
- [ ] Verify the plan design is built around the care your staff actually uses (first-dollar coverage on routine office visits, labs, generics, mental health, urgent care), not just a funding wrapper on a standard deductible plan.
- [ ] Confirm the claims reporting is itemized and usable, with pharmacy spend visible separately from medical.
- [ ] Confirm whether the pharmacy benefit can be carved out and re-shopped, and whether pricing is pass-through (clear fee, rebates passed back).
- [ ] Get the surplus-return terms in writing: what percentage, paid as cash or admin credit, and whether collecting it requires renewal.
- [ ] Verify claim funds are held per group with no commingling, so a healthy year is yours.
- [ ] Check ERISA treatment of surplus where employee contributions funded part of the premium (a slice may be plan assets owed back to participants, not free budget).
- [ ] Confirm the provider network actually covers your staff's geography before trusting an independent program's savings.
- [ ] Document the side-by-side comparison for the board record.

## Why this is standard practice for us and rare elsewhere

A generalist broker sells the carrier's packaged product because the quoting portal is familiar and the proposal arrives prefilled. Putting an independent program next to it is hours of work: knowing the administrators, reading a stop-loss contract he was not handed, and comparing pharmacy and surplus terms line by line. We do that work on every benefits placement, because for a charter staff with no HR department to absorb the difference, the structure underneath the label is where the money and the control actually live.

**Read the full breakdown:** [LINK TO WHITE PAPER]

---
