topics.title: Your Teachers Spent the Whole Year Paying for Benefits They Picked in a Forty-Minute Panic
topics.subtitle: Why the once-a-year paper scramble most charter schools call open enrollment quietly wastes the benefit dollars you already spent, and what running it like a real process looks like instead.

hub.content_markdown:
# Your Teachers Spent the Whole Year Paying for Benefits They Picked in a Forty-Minute Panic

*Why the once-a-year paper scramble most charter schools call open enrollment quietly wastes the benefit dollars you already spent, and what running it like a real process looks like instead.*

---

It's the third week of a packed month. Your office manager drops a stack of forms on every teacher's desk with a sticky note and a deadline. There's one all-staff meeting, squeezed into the end of a faculty day, where a binder gets flipped through and a slide deck gets rushed.

Most of your teachers do the only rational thing a tired person does with a confusing form and a hard stop. They check the same box they checked last year, or the first box on the page, and they get back to their classroom.

Then they spend the next twelve months paying for a plan nobody helped them understand, and quietly resenting a benefit you're spending real money on.

---

## What the scramble actually costs you

A school's benefit spend is one of its three biggest line items. You are pouring real budget into health coverage, and the way most schools run enrollment guarantees a chunk of that money lands with a thud instead of a payoff.

Run it as a once-a-year fire drill and you get four predictable problems. Engagement stays low, because nobody had time to engage. Plan choices stay poor, because a teacher with a new baby and a teacher about to retire both default into the same plan they had last year. Errors pile up, because somebody is hand-keying paper forms into a carrier portal at eleven at night. And compliance exposure grows, because rushed manual data entry is exactly where eligibility mistakes, missed deadlines, and bad dependent records come from.

Put those together and you've paid full price for benefits your staff don't understand, don't value, and in some cases didn't pick on purpose. That's the real bill. Not the premium. The premium you were always going to pay. The waste is spending it and getting resentment back.

## Why the paper scramble produces bad decisions

A benefit election is a real financial decision. A teacher is choosing how a family absorbs a medical bill for the next year. That decision deserves more than a form and a deadline.

When the only support is a thick binder and a forty-minute meeting, two things happen. First, the people who need help the most, the ones with a complicated year coming, are the ones least able to sort it out from a slide. Second, defaulting feels safe even when it isn't. Last year's plan might be wrong for this year's life, but it's the choice that requires zero thinking at 4 p.m. on a Friday. So people default, and they often default into the wrong thing.

That's not a staff problem. It's a process problem. You gave people a high-stakes choice and no tools to make it.

## What modern open enrollment actually means

Run well, open enrollment stops being an event and becomes a process that runs all year. Four pieces carry it.

**Enrollment technology that feeds clean data.** A benefits administration platform replaces the paper-and-portal handoff. Employees make elections in one place, the system enforces eligibility rules as they go, and clean data flows to the carriers instead of a tired human retyping it. That single change removes a whole category of enrollment errors and the compliance risk that rides along with them.

**Decision support, not just a plan list.** Good tools walk each employee through their own situation. Expecting a baby, managing a chronic condition, rarely see a doctor, the right plan is different for each, and the platform helps them see the real tradeoff between premium, deductible, and out-of-pocket exposure for their life, not the average life. People stop defaulting because, for the first time, the choice is legible.

**Communication in multiple formats, well before the deadline.** Not one meeting. A plan. Short videos, a one-page plain-language summary, a live session, an email sequence, all landing with enough runway that a teacher can take it home and talk it over with a spouse. You meet people where they are instead of where the binder is.

**Post-enrollment education so the benefit gets used.** Signing up is the start. People need to know how to actually use what they bought, how telehealth works, what their pharmacy benefit covers, where the no-cost preventive care is. A benefit nobody uses is a benefit nobody values, and you paid for it either way.

## Why this matters more for a charter school than almost anyone

You're competing for teachers against districts that often carry deep benefits and pension systems. Benefits are one of the few levers you fully control in that fight, and a sloppy enrollment process quietly blunts it. You spend the money and your staff walk away feeling like the benefit is confusing and thin, which is the opposite of what you paid for.

You also don't have a benefits department absorbing the chaos. You have an office manager or an HR person, often wearing four other hats, doing manual data entry and chasing down correction after correction. Every error that platform would have caught is an hour that person isn't spending on the actual job. The scramble doesn't just cost staff satisfaction. It costs you the time of the person holding the whole operation together.

## Where the gap really comes from

Here's the part worth being honest about. The reason most schools still run enrollment as a paper fire drill usually traces back to the broker.

A generalist benefits broker shows up once a year, drops off a binder, maybe sits in on the one meeting, and leaves. No enrollment platform stood up. No communication plan built. No decision-support tools put in front of staff. No follow-through after the deadline to make sure anyone understands what they signed. The renewal gets processed, the same plan gets re-upped, and the broker calls it service.

That's the easy button. Dropping a binder takes an afternoon. Building a real enrollment process takes work across the whole year, and a broker who treats your school as too small to fuss over isn't going to do that work. So the school inherits the scramble, year after year, and assumes that's just how enrollment goes.

It isn't. The schools that run it well aren't bigger or richer. They just have someone who treats enrollment as a process worth building instead of a form worth dropping off.

## What it looks like when someone's actually doing the job

A broker doing the work sets up the enrollment platform so elections are clean and the data reaches carriers without anyone retyping it. They build a communication calendar that starts weeks before the deadline, in formats your staff will actually open. They put decision-support tools in front of every employee so the choice fits the person. And they come back after enrollment closes to teach people how to use what they picked.

None of that is exotic. It's the difference between a broker who treats enrollment as an annual obligation and one who treats your benefit spend as money that should actually land with your staff.

## Before your next enrollment season

You don't need to overhaul anything today. You just need to look honestly at how last year went.

So here's the question worth sitting with:

When your staff made their benefit elections last year, how many of them actually chose a plan that fit their situation, and how many just checked last year's box because nobody gave them the time, the tools, or the plain-language help to do anything else? And if you can't answer that, who on your account was supposed to make sure they could?

If that question is uncomfortable to sit with, the problem was never your teachers. It was the process they were handed, and the person who handed it to them.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want enrollment to run like a real process instead of an annual scramble, the platform, the communication, the decision support, and the follow-through, that's the kind of thing we build before your next season starts.*

---

**Headline:** A teacher picked her family's health plan in forty rushed minutes, then paid for the wrong one for a year.

---

A teacher I'll never forget sat in a meeting just like that. New baby coming. Tired. Handed a confusing form and a hard stop. So she did the only rational thing a worn-out person does in that spot. She checked the same box she checked last year and got back to her classroom.

Then she spent the next twelve months paying for a plan nobody helped her understand, on a plan that fit last year's life and not this one. The school paid full freight for that coverage. She walked away feeling like the benefit was thin and confusing. Both things were true, and neither had to be.

I grew up around manual labor and a ranch, where doing a job halfway meant somebody got hurt or something broke, and you owned it. That's the standard I expected when I got into insurance. A lot of schools are not getting it.

I wrote the full breakdown up on our site, no jargon, no gate, free to read and free to forward to your business manager: **[LINK TO WHITE PAPER]**

Forward this to the school leader whose enrollment season starts soon.

Aaron Schwen

checklist.content_markdown:
# Company Page Spoke: Modern Open Enrollment Readiness Checklist

*Spoke type: Company storefront page (authoritative, technical, credibility-building). Goal: validate Charter Select to a prospect actively vetting us. Post from the Charter Select company page. Lead with the matrix, then the checklist.*

---

**Headline:** If your open enrollment is one binder and one meeting, you are paying full premium for benefits your staff don't understand and don't use.

Benefit spend is one of the three largest line items a charter school carries. The way enrollment gets run decides whether that money lands with your staff or lands with a thud. Below is how the annual scramble compares to enrollment run as a year-round process, and the checklist we work through before every renewal season.

## Annual scramble vs. enrollment run as a process

| Element | The annual scramble | Enrollment run as a process | Why the gap costs you |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Enrollment mechanics** | Paper forms hand-keyed into a carrier portal | Benefits admin platform with eligibility rules enforced at entry | Manual entry is where eligibility errors, missed deadlines, and bad dependent records start |
| **Plan selection** | One binder, one slide deck, default to last year's box | Decision support tailored to each employee's situation | A new parent and a near-retiree default into the same plan that fits neither |
| **Communication** | A single meeting at the end of a faculty day | Multi-format plan with runway before the deadline | People can't engage with a choice they were given no time to consider |
| **After enrollment** | Nothing | Education on how to actually use the coverage | A benefit nobody uses is a benefit nobody values |
| **Compliance** | Exposure from rushed manual data entry | Clean data flowing straight to carriers | Rushed entry is the root of most enrollment compliance risk |

You don't need every element perfect. You need the process pointed in the right direction before your season starts, not a binder dropped on a desk.

## The pre-enrollment readiness checklist

- [ ] Confirm whether elections run through a benefits administration platform or still move on paper.
- [ ] Verify the platform enforces eligibility rules at the point of entry, not after the fact.
- [ ] Confirm clean election data flows to carriers without anyone retyping it.
- [ ] Put decision-support tools in front of every employee so the choice fits the person, not the average.
- [ ] Build a communication calendar that starts weeks before the deadline, in formats staff will actually open.
- [ ] Include a plain-language one-page summary, a live session, and an email sequence in that calendar.
- [ ] Give staff enough runway to take the choice home and discuss it before deciding.
- [ ] Schedule post-enrollment education on telehealth, pharmacy benefits, and no-cost preventive care.
- [ ] Confirm who owns each of these steps on your account, in writing, before the season opens.

## Why this is standard practice for us and rare elsewhere

A generalist broker drops off a binder, sits in on one meeting, processes the renewal, and calls it service. That takes an afternoon. Building enrollment that runs all year takes work across the whole year, and a broker who treats a school as too small to fuss over won't do it. We stand up the platform, build the communication calendar, put decision support in front of every employee, and come back after the deadline to teach people how to use what they picked, because your benefit spend should land with your staff instead of leaving them resentful of a benefit you paid full price for.

**Read the full breakdown:** [LINK TO WHITE PAPER]

---

*Posting notes: this is the credibility asset. Keep it clean and technical. The checklist also works as a downloadable PDF lead-capture on the website (top/bottom of the hub page). Replace [LINK TO WHITE PAPER] with the live URL.*

hub.content_markdown:
# The Same Pill, Two Schools, A Price Gap Wide Enough To Hire A Teacher

*Pharmacy Benefit Managers, made to make sense. How the middleman in your drug benefit gets paid, why the same medication can cost wildly different amounts depending on who runs the program, and the levers a real strategy pulls to bring the number down.*

---

Two charter schools fill the exact same prescription in the same month. One plan gets billed a few dollars. The other gets billed a few hundred for the identical molecule, same manufacturer, same dose.

Neither principal saw the difference. Neither one was told it existed.

The thing that decided it wasn't the drug. It was the contract sitting behind the pharmacy counter.

---

## The middleman you've never met

Every drug benefit you offer runs through a company called a Pharmacy Benefit Manager, a PBM. The PBM builds your covered-drug list, negotiates with manufacturers, sets what each pharmacy gets paid, and decides what your plan gets charged. Three large PBMs control the majority of the prescription claims processed in the United States, so most plans, including the one stapled to your fully insured renewal, ride on one of a small handful of them.

The pitch is simple. The PBM is the buyer with scale, so it gets you better prices than you could get alone. Sometimes that's true. The problem is the way most legacy contracts are written, because under a traditional structure the middleman makes more money when your drug spend goes up, not down. SmithRx, a transparent PBM, has a name for this design. They call it inverse incentives: the vendor profits when your spending soars. The incentive points the wrong way, and it points straight at your budget.

Let me walk through the mechanics that do the damage, then the model that fixes them, then the levers a real pharmacy strategy uses to cut the bill.

## Mechanic one: spread pricing

Spread pricing is the cleanest example of the wrong incentive.

The PBM pays the pharmacy one price for a drug. It charges your plan a higher price. It keeps the difference. That difference is the spread, and you almost never see it, because the contract shows you one number, the price billed to the plan, and never the number paid to the pharmacy underneath it.

The math is small per claim and enormous in aggregate. If a pharmacy gets reimbursed two dollars for a generic and your plan gets billed two dollars and fifty cents, the PBM books fifty cents of pure margin. Run that across a staff of a hundred people filling maintenance prescriptions month after month, year after year, and the spread becomes real money that quietly leaves your plan with no line item attached to it.

You can't manage a number you can't see. That's the entire design.

## Mechanic two: rebate retention

Drug manufacturers pay rebates to PBMs to win favorable placement on the formulary, the list of drugs the plan covers and how it covers them. Better placement means more volume, so manufacturers pay to get there.

Here's where it bends. A rebate is usually structured as a percentage off the drug's list price, so the higher the list price, the bigger the rebate dollars. A traditional PBM that keeps a share of those rebates therefore has a quiet reason to favor the expensive drug over the cheaper one, because the pricier product can pay the larger rebate. The system can steer toward a high-cost brand when a lower-cost option would have worked fine, and the rebate that's supposed to be a discount for your plan becomes a revenue stream for the middleman instead.

So you can end up paying for a more expensive drug than your people needed, while a meaningful share of the rebate that justified it never reaches your school.

## Mechanic three: the fees you can't find on the invoice

Spread and rebates are the famous two. They are not the whole game. SmithRx catalogs a longer list of ways legacy contracts pull money out without showing it, and the pattern is worth knowing before you read your own contract.

There are clawbacks, where the patient's copay collects more than the drug actually cost the plan and the PBM recovers the excess. There are DIR fees, direct and indirect remuneration, charged to the pharmacy after the sale, so a price quoted at the counter quietly grows later. And there are the administrative fees themselves, which can look modest on page one and balloon once the surcharges are counted. SmithRx points to a tidy example: a fee advertised around four dollars per member per month that lands above ten once every ad-hoc charge is added in. Tie all of this together with the formulary, which in a traditional model isn't a neutral medical document, it's a profit instrument, and you have a system tuned to maximize what the middleman keeps rather than to land your members on the most effective drug at the lowest true cost.

## The model that fixes it: transparent, pass-through, aligned

There's a structurally different way to run a PBM, and once you see it, the legacy version stops making sense.

A transparent, pass-through PBM gets paid one way: a clear, disclosed administrative fee. It charges your plan exactly what the pharmacy was paid, so there's no spread to hide. It passes one hundred percent of manufacturer rebates back to the plan, so the discount actually lands with you. And it gives you real visibility into the true cost of every drug, which means you can finally manage the spend instead of guessing at it.

The founder's experience points to SmithRx as a strong example of this model, and it's worth being specific about what they actually do, because the specifics are the lesson. Their pricing reimburses each pharmacy for its real acquisition cost, using public market proxies like NADAC when a direct number isn't available, plus a fixed dispensing fee. Their revenue comes from a flat monthly fee per member, not a cut of the drug, and not a charge per claim. That last detail matters more than it sounds: a per-claim model quietly rewards more transactions, while a flat per-member fee gives the PBM no reason to want your people filling more prescriptions or pricier ones. They remit one hundred percent of manufacturer rebates and discounts to the plan. And the number they steer the whole program toward isn't the biggest rebate, it's the lowest net cost: total drug spend, minus every rebate, measured as a per-member-per-month figure you can actually watch. Chasing lowest net cost pushes a formulary toward low-cost generics and biosimilars instead of the brand that happened to pay the fattest rebate.

That's an example of the type, based on what the founder has seen work for clients, not a claim that one vendor beats every other on every metric. The point is the architecture. When the fee is fixed and disclosed, the spread is gone, the rebates flow through, and the scoreboard is net cost, the hidden revenue streams a traditional contract depends on simply aren't there, and the spend tends to drift down because nobody's being steered toward the expensive option anymore.

There's a quieter benefit too, the one your staff feels. A transparent program puts the data in reach: claims you can see, a member tool that finds the nearest pharmacy carrying a drug at the lowest covered price, prior-authorization status a member can actually track instead of waiting on a black box. Transparency isn't only a finance feature. It shows up at the counter.

## The levers a real strategy pulls

A good pharmacy strategy doesn't stop at clean pricing. It actively attacks the biggest line items, especially specialty drugs, where a single member's medication can cost more than several teachers' worth of premium. Three levers do most of the work. Each one is legitimate, and each one has a catch you have to respect.

**Manufacturer copay assistance.** Drug makers offer copay cards that cut a member's out-of-pocket cost on a brand-name medication. Used well, they ease the burden on the member and can pull dollars off the plan. The caution: copay cards tend to favor brand drugs over cheaper generics, and many plans run copay accumulator or maximizer programs that accept the manufacturer's payment but don't count it toward the member's deductible or out-of-pocket maximum. When the card's annual limit runs dry mid-year, the member can get hit with the full cost they thought they'd already met. Some states have started forcing plans to count that assistance, but the rules are uneven, so the tool only helps if your program is built to capture it cleanly.

**Patient assistance programs.** For high-cost specialty drugs, manufacturers and nonprofit foundations run patient assistance programs, or PAPs, that can provide the medication free or at deep discount to members who qualify. For a single expensive specialty case, a PAP can take a five- or six-figure annual drug off the plan entirely. A transparent PBM will often build this in as a service rather than leave it to chance: SmithRx, for instance, runs a specialty navigation program that helps members find and apply to advocacy and grant foundations when a high-cost drug isn't covered under the standard benefit. The caution: these programs carry income limits and eligibility rules, so they don't apply to everyone, and a strategy that leans on them has to handle the enrollment and qualification work for real, not just point at the program and hope.

**International and global sourcing.** Some programs source certain brand and high-cost specialty drugs from abroad, where the same medication can cost a fraction of the U.S. price. The potential savings on a handful of specialty drugs are large. The caution here is the heaviest of the three, so be precise about it. The FDA has flagged commercial importation of U.S.-approved drugs as a legal gray area at best, with real concerns about a medication's supply chain, labeling, storage conditions, and whether it was made at an approved facility. Reputable programs limit themselves: no controlled substances, no drugs under FDA REMS safety programs, supply caps, and they keep it voluntary for the member. This lever can work, but only inside guardrails, with eyes open to the regulatory and safety questions. Anyone who pitches it as a free lunch is not being straight with you.

## Why your school is overpaying right now

None of this is exotic, and none of it is reserved for big corporations. Schools your size run transparent pass-through programs today. The reason yours probably doesn't comes down to who's on your account.

Picture what your benefits broker actually does. The PBM came bundled inside the carrier's fully insured plan. The broker handed you that plan, renewed it every year, walked you through the premium increase, and called it service. Nobody ever explained what a PBM was, what spread pricing meant, or where the rebate dollars went. A generalist carrying restaurants and law firms and your school in the same book has no reason to crack open a pharmacy contract and dissect it. The easy button is to re-up what already exists and move on. So the bundled PBM keeps taking the spread, keeps the rebates that should have been yours, keeps the fees you'd never find on the invoice, and the school overpays for years without a single number on a statement to explain why.

Doing this right is a different job. It means carving the pharmacy benefit out, putting a transparent pass-through PBM behind it, holding the program to a lowest-net-cost standard, and layering in the copay, PAP, and sourcing levers where they fit and where they're safe. That work doesn't happen at a renewal meeting the night before the policy expires. It happens because someone decided your school was worth the dig.

## Before your next renewal

You don't need to become a pharmacy expert to find out where you stand. You need to ask one honest question and watch how quickly it gets answered.

So sit with this. When your plan pays for a prescription, does anyone on your account know the difference between what the pharmacy was paid and what your plan was billed? Can they show you, in writing, whether the rebates your drugs earned came back to your school or stayed with the middleman, and what every fee on that contract actually totals once the surcharges are added? And if the answer is a shrug, who decided that the bundled PBM nobody ever explained to you was the best your people and your budget could do?

If nobody ever sat down and built the pharmacy benefit on purpose, you don't have a drug-cost problem. You have a question nobody on your account has bothered to ask.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want a straight read on what your current PBM is actually charging you, and whether a transparent pass-through setup would lower the spend, that's the kind of digging we do before anyone talks about switching a plan.*

newsletter.content_markdown:
# LinkedIn Personal Newsletter: The PBM Deep Dive

*Spoke type: Personal profile newsletter (Aaron's voice). Goal: human story + compressed insight, driving to the full white paper. Post from Aaron's personal LinkedIn. ~750 words.*

---

**Headline:** Two schools filled the same prescription the same month. One was billed a few dollars. The other a few hundred. Same pill.

---

I pulled two charter school plans side by side last year and found something that still bothers me.

Same drug. Same manufacturer. Same dose. Filled in the same month. One plan got billed a few dollars for it. The other got billed a few hundred. Nobody at either school had any idea. Neither principal had ever been shown the difference, and neither one had been told the difference even existed.

The thing that decided the price wasn't the medication. It was a contract sitting behind the pharmacy counter that nobody on either account had ever read.

Every drug benefit you offer runs through a company called a Pharmacy Benefit Manager. A PBM. It builds your covered-drug list, decides what each pharmacy gets paid, and decides what your plan gets charged. The sales pitch is that the PBM is the big buyer with scale, so it gets you better prices than you could get on your own. Sometimes that holds. The problem is how most of these contracts are written, because under the traditional structure the middleman makes more money when your drug spend goes up, not down. SmithRx, a transparent PBM, calls this inverse incentives. The vendor profits when your spending climbs. The incentive points straight at your budget.

Here is how the money actually moves.

There is spread pricing. The PBM pays the pharmacy one price for a drug, charges your plan a higher price, and keeps the difference. You never see it, because the contract shows you one number and hides the one underneath it. Fifty cents on a generic sounds like nothing. Run it across a staff of a hundred people filling maintenance scripts month after month and it becomes real money that leaves your plan with no line item attached.

There are rebates. Manufacturers pay PBMs to get their drug placed well on your formulary. A traditional PBM that keeps a share of those rebates has a quiet reason to favor the expensive drug over the cheaper one, because the pricier product pays the bigger rebate. So you can end up covering a costlier medication than your people needed, while the discount that justified it never reaches your school.

Then there are the fees you would never find on the invoice. Clawbacks. DIR fees charged to the pharmacy after the sale. Administrative fees advertised around four dollars a member that quietly land north of ten once every surcharge is counted. The whole thing is tuned to maximize what the middleman keeps, not to land your people on the right drug at the lowest real cost.

There is a structurally different way to run this. A transparent, pass-through PBM gets paid one way: a flat, disclosed fee. It charges your plan exactly what the pharmacy was paid, so there is no spread to hide. It passes one hundred percent of rebates back to the plan. And it steers the whole program toward one number, lowest net cost, which is total drug spend minus every rebate, measured per member per month. Chase that number and the formulary drifts toward low-cost generics and biosimilars instead of whatever paid the fattest rebate. The spend tends to fall, because nobody is being pushed toward the expensive option anymore.

I grew up around manual labor and a ranch, where a job done halfway got somebody hurt or broke something, and you owned it. That is the standard I expected when I got into insurance. A bundled PBM that quietly takes the spread, keeps the rebates that were yours, and buries the fees is the opposite of that standard, and it has been running on charter schools for years.

The reason yours probably looks like this comes down to who is on your account. The PBM came stapled inside the carrier's fully insured plan. Your broker handed you that plan, renewed it every year, walked you through the premium hike, and called it service. A generalist carrying restaurants and law firms in the same book has no reason to crack open a pharmacy contract and take it apart. That is most of why Charter Select only works with charter schools and actually does the dig.

I wrote the full breakdown on our site. No jargon, no gate, free to read and free to forward to your board or your business manager: **[LINK TO WHITE PAPER]**

If you do one thing with this, do this. Ask whoever runs your benefits a single question. When your plan pays for a prescription, does anyone on your account know the difference between what the pharmacy was paid and what your plan was billed, and can they show you in writing whether your rebates came back to your school or stayed with the middleman?

If the answer is a shrug, you don't have a drug-cost problem. You have a question nobody on your account has bothered to ask.

Aaron Schwen

---

*Posting notes: replace [LINK TO WHITE PAPER] with the live hub URL. Keep the sign-off personal. First comment should drop the link again (LinkedIn suppresses posts with outbound links in the body, so some people put the link in the first comment instead, test both).*

checklist.label: Company-page checklist / matrix
checklist.content_markdown:
# Company Page Spoke: Transparent Pass-Through PBM vs Traditional Spread-and-Rebate PBM Checklist

*Spoke type: Company storefront page (authoritative, technical, credibility-building). Goal: validate Charter Select to a prospect actively vetting us. Post from the Charter Select company page. Lead with the matrix, then the checklist.*

---

**Headline:** Your drug benefit runs on a PBM contract you have probably never read. Here is how to tell whether it is built for your budget or the middleman's.

Every prescription your plan covers is priced by a Pharmacy Benefit Manager. Two PBMs can charge wildly different amounts for the identical drug, and the difference is the contract structure, not the medication. Below is how a transparent pass-through model compares to a traditional spread-and-rebate model on the points that decide your spend, followed by the review we run on every pharmacy benefit.

## Transparent pass-through vs traditional spread-and-rebate PBM

| Lever | Traditional spread-and-rebate PBM | Transparent pass-through PBM |
|---|---|---|
| **How the PBM gets paid** | Spread, retained rebates, and per-claim fees, so revenue rises with your spend | One flat, disclosed fee per member per month |
| **Drug pricing to your plan** | Marked up above what the pharmacy was paid; you see one number | Exactly what the pharmacy was paid, using proxies like NADAC plus a fixed dispensing fee |
| **Manufacturer rebates** | A share is retained by the PBM | 100% passed through to the plan |
| **Formulary incentive** | Can favor the high-list-price drug that pays the bigger rebate | Steered to lowest net cost, favoring generics and biosimilars |
| **Hidden charges** | Clawbacks, DIR fees, admin fees that balloon with surcharges | Disclosed; no spread, no per-claim cut to hide |
| **The scoreboard** | Biggest rebate | Lowest net cost (total spend minus every rebate, per member per month) |
| **Member visibility** | Black-box claims and prior-auth status | Claims you can see, pharmacy price-finder, trackable prior-auth |

A traditional structure profits when your drug spend goes up. A pass-through structure makes the same flat fee whether your people fill more scripts or fewer, which removes the reason to steer them toward the expensive option.

## The pharmacy benefit checklist

- [ ] Confirm whether your PBM is a transparent pass-through or a traditional spread-and-rebate model.
- [ ] Ask, in writing, for the difference between what the pharmacy was paid and what your plan was billed on a sample of claims.
- [ ] Confirm what share of manufacturer rebates flows back to the plan, and get the number in writing.
- [ ] Total every administrative and ad-hoc fee in the contract, not just the headline PMPM rate.
- [ ] Ask how the formulary is built: lowest net cost, or biggest rebate.
- [ ] Confirm the PBM is paid a flat per-member fee, not a per-claim fee that rewards more transactions.
- [ ] On specialty drugs, confirm the program runs copay assistance, patient assistance programs (PAPs), and any sourcing levers inside real guardrails, not as a free lunch.
- [ ] Establish a lowest-net-cost PMPM figure you can actually watch quarter to quarter.

## Why this is standard practice for us

A generalist broker inherits the PBM bundled inside the carrier's fully insured plan, renews it every year, and never opens the pharmacy contract. We carve the pharmacy benefit out, put a transparent pass-through PBM behind it, hold the program to a lowest-net-cost standard, and layer in the specialty levers where they fit and where they are safe. That work does not happen at a renewal meeting the night before the policy expires. It happens because someone decided your school was worth the dig.

**Read the full breakdown:** [LINK TO WHITE PAPER]

---

*Posting notes: this is the credibility asset. Keep it clean and technical. The checklist also works as a downloadable PDF lead-capture on the website (top/bottom of the hub page). Replace [LINK TO WHITE PAPER] with the live URL.*

topics.title: Your Staff Member Handed Over the Insurance Card and the Plan Paid More Than the Cash Price
topics.subtitle: A plain-English look at hospital price transparency and price variance: why the same procedure costs wildly different amounts at different facilities, why paying more does not buy better care, and why the network "discount" your school renews every year is measured off a number nobody should pay.

hub.content_markdown:
# Your Staff Member Handed Over the Insurance Card and the Plan Paid More Than the Cash Price

*A plain-English look at hospital price transparency and price variance: why the same procedure costs wildly different amounts at different facilities, why paying more does not buy better care, and why the network "discount" your school renews every year is measured off a number nobody should pay.*

---

A staff member needs an MRI. They do the responsible thing and show their insurance card.

The plan gets billed the network rate, pays its share, and the member pays a copay. Everyone assumes the card saved money. That is what the card is for.

Down the road, a clinic posts a cash price for the exact same scan. It is lower than what your plan just paid. The card did not save anyone money. It cost the plan more, and it cost the member more.

That is not a rare glitch. Federal data now shows it happening across thousands of hospitals, and most school leaders have never been shown the numbers.

---

## The same procedure, a different price at every door

Start with the part that breaks most people's mental model of how healthcare pricing works. There is no single price for a medical service. Not even close.

Researchers digging into the price data hospitals are now required to publish have found enormous spread for identical care. One analysis documented price variation of more than ten times for the same procedure inside a single hospital, depending on which payer was paying, and more than thirty times across different hospitals in the same state. Same scan. Same blood test. Same outpatient surgery. The price swings by a factor of ten, twenty, thirty, based mostly on who is paying and where.

A school leader hears that and assumes the expensive facility must be the better one. You are paying for quality. That assumption is where a lot of plan money goes to die.

## Paying more does not buy better care

The link between price and quality in American healthcare is weak at best, and in plenty of studies it is not there at all. A higher price tag does not reliably mean a better surgeon, a lower complication rate, or a better outcome. Researchers who have tried to map cost against common quality measures keep landing in the same uncomfortable place: the correlation is murky, frequently absent, and nowhere near strong enough to justify treating a high price as a proxy for good care.

Read that plainly. Your plan can pay three times more at one facility than another for the same procedure and get the same result, or a worse one. The premium price is buying location and leverage, not skill.

So if price does not track quality, what is it tracking? Mostly negotiating power and a starting number that was rigged high from the beginning.

## The "discount" is measured off a fake number

Here is the mechanic the network plan is built to keep quiet.

A hospital's list price lives in a document called the chargemaster. Chargemaster prices are not tied to what care costs to deliver. They are set high on purpose, as an opening position. When your PPO network advertises that it negotiated a 40 or 50 percent discount, that discount comes off the chargemaster. You are getting a real percentage off an unreal number.

A markup study of fourteen common hospital services put the chargemaster ratios at roughly three to eleven times a reasonable cost basis, with negotiated prices still running several times higher than that basis. The discount is real. The price it discounts from is theater. Cut a fake number in half and you can still land far above what the service is actually worth.

This matters more than any other line in your health plan, because hospital and facility spend is the single heaviest line in almost every plan. The place you are most exposed is the exact place the pricing is most rigged.

## The card can cost you more than cash

Now the finding that should stop a busy administrator cold.

The cash price, the number a hospital posts for someone paying without insurance, is frequently lower than the price your insurance negotiated. A study published in Health Affairs, using the cash and negotiated prices hospitals are now required to disclose for seventy shoppable services across more than two thousand hospitals, found that for nearly half of those services, roughly 47 percent, the cash price was lower than or equal to the median insurance-negotiated price at the same hospital, for the same service, in the same setting.

Sit with what that means. In a large share of cases, a member who shows the insurance card pays a price the plan negotiated that is higher than the price they would have gotten by paying cash and never mentioning insurance. The plan pays more. The member's coinsurance is calculated off the bigger number, so the member pays more too. The card, the thing everyone assumes is protection, becomes the more expensive option.

For a self-funded school plan, that is not an abstraction. Every dollar of inflated negotiated price is your plan's dollar, which is your budget's dollar.

## The question worth actually reasoning through

Here is where it gets provocative, and I want to reason through it rather than hand you a slogan.

A big part of what a broad network buys you is access to negotiated rates and out-of-network coverage. But if the negotiated rate is frequently higher than the cash price, and if quality does not track price, then for some plans the honest question is what that broad network access is really buying. Are you paying a premium for access to genuinely lower prices, or paying for access to inflated prices dressed up as discounts?

I am not telling you to tear up your network tomorrow. Networks still do real work, especially for catastrophic and complex care where a single huge claim is the thing that matters, and out-of-network protection has value you should not casually throw away. But the reflex that broad network access is automatically worth its cost deserves a hard look with your own numbers. For some plan designs, paying transparent cash-level prices on the shoppable, high-volume services, while keeping protection where it counts, is the cheaper and cleaner path. That is a question to run the data on, not a thing to assume in either direction.

This is also where reference-based pricing enters the conversation, as one downstream tool. RBP anchors what the plan pays to a transparent benchmark like Medicare instead of a chargemaster discount. It is one way to act on everything above. It is not the only one, and it is a separate paper.

## Why you can finally see any of this

None of these numbers used to be visible. Hospitals kept their prices, insurers kept their negotiated rates, and the whole thing was a black box. That changed.

The federal Hospital Price Transparency rule took effect January 1, 2021. It requires every hospital in the country to publish a machine-readable file listing its standard charges, including gross charges, the discounted cash price, and payer-specific negotiated charges. On top of that, the Transparency in Coverage rule requires health plans and insurers to post machine-readable files of their in-network negotiated rates and out-of-network allowed amounts, with those files required for plan years beginning on or after July 1, 2022.

The data is messy and the files are huge, and a busy school leader is never going to parse them alone. But the point stands. The information that exposes price variance and the cash-versus-negotiated gap is now public and required by law. Somebody just has to actually look at it on your behalf.

## What a broker who's working would have shown you

A broker doing the job pulls the transparency data for the facilities your staff actually use, lays the cash price next to the negotiated price on your highest-volume services, and shows you where your plan is paying the big number for no extra quality. They treat the heaviest line in your plan as something to interrogate, not rubber-stamp.

A generalist does none of that. The easy-button renewal is to re-up the same network, repeat the line about the negotiated discount, pass along the increase, and move to the next account. The transparency files never get opened. The cash-versus-negotiated comparison never gets run. Your school keeps paying inflated prices off a chargemaster while the real numbers sit published on a public website nobody on your account bothered to read.

## Before your next renewal

You do not need to become a pricing analyst. You need one honest look at whether your plan is paying real prices or rigged ones.

So here is the question worth sitting with:

On the handful of procedures your staff use most, has anyone on your account ever pulled the published cash price and set it next to what your plan actually paid through the network? If no one has put those two numbers side by side, how would you know whether your network is buying you lower prices, or just charging you for access to higher ones?

If you cannot answer that, you do not have a verdict yet. You have a question nobody on your account has bothered to ask, on data that has been public for years.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want the published cash and negotiated prices for the facilities your people actually use laid side by side with what your plan paid, that's the kind of look we run before recommending a single change.*

checklist.label: Company-page checklist / matrix
checklist.content_markdown:
# Company Page Spoke: Cash vs. Negotiated Price Variance Review Checklist

*Spoke type: Company storefront page (authoritative, technical, credibility-building). Goal: validate Charter Select to a prospect actively vetting us. Post from the Charter Select company page. Lead with the matrix, then the checklist.*

---

**Headline:** Before you renew the same network, see how its "discount" actually stacks up against the published cash price. On the high-volume services, the gap is often the wrong direction.

Hospital and facility spend is the single heaviest line in almost every health plan, and since 2021 the prices behind it have been public and required by law. Most plans never get the comparison run. Here is how the four pricing reference points relate to each other, and the review we run on a plan's highest-volume shoppable services before recommending a single change.

## The four numbers in play on any procedure

| Price reference | What it is | How it is set | What to do with it |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Chargemaster / gross charge** | The hospital's list price | Set high on purpose as an opening position; not tied to delivery cost | Treat as theater. The number every "discount" is measured against. |
| **Negotiated (network) rate** | What your PPO negotiated | A percentage off the chargemaster | Verify it against the cash price; a discount off a rigged number can still run high. |
| **Cash / self-pay price** | What the hospital posts for no-insurance payers | Published per the Hospital Price Transparency rule | Compare to the negotiated rate on your top services. Lower in roughly 47% of cases. |
| **Transparent benchmark (e.g., Medicare-referenced)** | A cost-based anchor | Tied to a public reference, not a chargemaster | The basis for reference-based pricing if the data supports a move. |

You cannot tell whether your network is buying lower prices or selling access to higher ones until those numbers sit side by side. Renewing without that comparison is renewing blind on your biggest line.

## The pre-renewal review checklist

- [ ] Identify the plan's highest-volume shoppable services (imaging, common labs, routine outpatient procedures).
- [ ] List the facilities your staff actually use, not a generic regional average.
- [ ] Pull each facility's published cash price for those services from its machine-readable transparency file.
- [ ] Pull the plan's negotiated rate for the same service, same facility, same setting.
- [ ] Flag every service where the cash price is at or below the negotiated rate.
- [ ] Quantify annual plan exposure: per-service gap multiplied by utilization.
- [ ] Confirm price is not standing in for quality (price-quality correlation is weak; do not assume the high number buys a better outcome).
- [ ] Decide where network access earns its cost (catastrophic and complex care) versus where transparent cash-level pricing wins (high-volume shoppable services).
- [ ] If the data supports it, evaluate reference-based pricing as one downstream tool, scoped in a separate analysis.
- [ ] Document the comparison for the board record before binding the renewal.

## Why this is standard practice for us and rare elsewhere

A generalist renews the same network, repeats the line about the negotiated discount, passes along the increase, and never opens the transparency files. We open them. We lay the published cash price next to what the plan actually paid on the services your people use most, and we show you where the plan is paying the big number for no extra quality. The information that exposes the gap has been public for years. Someone just has to look at it on your behalf, and that look comes before we recommend anything.

**Read the full breakdown:** [LINK TO WHITE PAPER]

---

topics.title: The Same Surgery Costs Your Plan Three Times What the Hospital Will Accept. You Just Paid the Big Number.
topics.subtitle: A plain-English look at reference-based pricing: how a self-funded school can pay hospital bills off a transparent benchmark instead of an inflated chargemaster, what it really saves, and the tradeoff anyone honest has to tell you about.

hub.content_markdown:
# The Same Surgery Costs Your Plan Three Times What the Hospital Will Accept. You Just Paid the Big Number.

*A plain-English look at reference-based pricing: how a self-funded school can pay hospital bills off a transparent benchmark instead of an inflated chargemaster, what it really saves, and the tradeoff anyone honest has to tell you about.*

---

A staff member has surgery. The hospital bills your plan a number with a lot of zeros. Your plan pays it, minus the discount your network negotiated, and everyone moves on.

Now picture the same procedure at the same hospital, billed to Medicare. The hospital accepts a fraction of what your plan just paid. Same operating room, same surgeon, same supplies. A wildly different price, and the lower one still keeps the lights on at the hospital.

Your plan paid the big number. Most school leaders have never been shown there was another one.

---

## What you're actually paying for in a network plan

The health plan most schools carry pays providers through a PPO network. The pitch is simple and it sounds great. The network negotiates a discount off the hospital's billed charges, and your plan pays the discounted amount.

The problem hides in the words "billed charges." A hospital's list price lives in a document called the chargemaster, and chargemaster prices are not tied to what care costs to deliver. They are set high on purpose. A 40 percent or 50 percent discount off a number that was inflated to begin with can still land well above what the same service is worth.

So the network discount, the thing that feels like the win, is a discount off a price nobody should be paying in the first place. You can negotiate hard against a fake number and still overpay. That is the quiet design of network pricing, and it is the single biggest line in your health plan: hospital and facility costs.

## Reference-based pricing flips the starting point

Reference-based pricing, usually shortened to RBP, attacks that problem at its root. Instead of starting from the hospital's inflated list price and discounting down, the plan starts from a transparent, objective benchmark and pays up from there.

The benchmark most RBP plans use is Medicare. Medicare already calculates, service by service, a defined reimbursement rate that hospitals accept every day. RBP takes that rate and pays a set multiple of it. A plan might reimburse, for example, a fixed percentage above Medicare for a given procedure, commonly somewhere in the range of 120 to 200 percent of the Medicare rate depending on the plan design and the service.

Sit with the gap for a second. Industry analyses of commercial network pricing frequently put it at several times Medicare, sometimes 300 percent or more. An RBP plan anchored at, say, 150 percent of Medicare is paying the hospital well above its Medicare rate, which is a fair and sustainable price, while paying far less than the network would have. The savings come from anchoring payment to an objective cost basis instead of an opaque list price.

That is the whole idea. You stop arguing about the discount and start defining the price.

## What this can actually save

Be careful with savings numbers, because vendors love to wave around the best-case figure. Spoken honestly: published industry estimates commonly describe self-funded employers cutting total health plan spend by something in the range of 20 to 30 percent after moving to a well-run RBP arrangement. Your school's actual result depends on your claims, your region, and how the program is run. Treat any single percentage as a marketing number until someone runs your real data.

The reason the savings can be that large is simple. Hospital and facility spend is the heaviest line in almost every health plan. Cut the price basis on the heaviest line and the whole plan moves. Trimming around the edges of a network plan never touches that.

## This only works on a self-funded chassis

RBP is not a plan you bolt onto a fully insured policy. It belongs on a self-funded or level-funded plan, the same chassis covered in the funding conversation. The reason is structural. In a self-funded arrangement, the plan, meaning your school, decides what it pays for each claim. That authority is exactly what RBP uses. The plan defines its allowable amount off the Medicare benchmark, and a third-party administrator processes claims and pays providers at that defined amount.

If you are still fully insured, the insurer sets the price and keeps the upside, so there is no lever for you to pull. RBP is one of the concrete reasons the move off fully insured matters. It opens a door that stays locked otherwise.

## Now the tradeoff, said out loud

Anyone selling RBP as free money is not being straight with you, so here is the honest part.

Because an RBP plan pays off a benchmark instead of a signed network contract, there is no contract stopping a hospital from sending the member a bill for the difference between its list price and what the plan paid. That is called balance billing, and it is the real risk of this model. It is the thing the slick pitch decks gloss over.

This risk is manageable, and a serious RBP program manages it as a core feature, not an afterthought. Good programs build in member advocacy and balance-bill defense. When a provider sends a balance bill, a patient advocacy and legal team steps in on the member's behalf, engages the provider, and works to resolve the charge so the employee is not left holding it. Reputable administrators report that the large majority of claims end with no balance bill to the member, but "large majority" is not "never," and you should distrust anyone who promises never.

There are two more real frictions. Some providers, particularly large hospital systems, push back on RBP and may be less willing to treat plan members smoothly or may require more coordination up front. And the whole thing depends on employee communication. Your staff need to understand the plan before they get a surprising bill in the mail, and they need to know exactly who to call the moment one shows up. An RBP plan with weak member support is a complaint factory. An RBP plan with strong advocacy is a cost-control engine your staff barely notice.

## Why your school was never shown this

Here is the pattern. Reference-based pricing is more work to design, more work to explain to staff, and more work to support once it is running. It requires a broker who will sit with your people, set up the advocacy and defense services correctly, and stay in the room when a member calls about a bill.

A generalist benefits broker does none of that. The easy-button renewal is to re-up the same network plan, pass along the increase, and move on to the next account. RBP never comes up, not because it would not fit your school, but because it would ask something of the broker. So the heaviest, most attackable line in your entire health plan goes unexamined year after year, and you keep paying the big number while a fair number was sitting right there.

That is not a knock on every broker. It is a description of what happens when nobody on your account treats your health spend as something to actively manage.

## Before your next renewal

You do not need to commit to anything to find out whether this fits. You need one honest look at where your money goes.

So here is the question worth sitting with:

On your largest hospital and facility claims last year, has anyone ever shown you what those services would have cost benchmarked against Medicare, next to what your plan actually paid? If no one has ever put those two numbers side by side for you, how would you know whether you have a cost problem, or just a broker who never looked?

If you cannot answer that, you do not have a verdict yet. You have a question nobody on your account has bothered to ask on your behalf.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want to see what your real hospital claims would have cost on a transparent benchmark, with the balance-billing risk explained straight and the member-advocacy piece built in, that's the analysis we run before recommending a single change.*

newsletter.content_markdown:
# LinkedIn Personal Newsletter: Reference-Based Pricing

*Spoke type: Personal profile newsletter (Aaron's voice). Goal: human story + compressed insight, driving to the full white paper. Post from Aaron's personal LinkedIn. ~750 words.*

---

**Headline:** A staff member had surgery. The hospital would have taken a fraction of what your plan paid. Nobody ever showed the school the smaller number.

---

A business manager at a charter school walked me through her health plan last year. Costs were up again. The renewal letter blamed claims, blamed the market, blamed everything except the one thing nobody had ever put in front of her.

So I asked her about a single surgery on her plan from the prior year. A real claim, a real staff member, a procedure with a lot of zeros attached. Her plan had paid it, minus the network discount, and everyone moved on.

Then I asked a different question. What would that same hospital have accepted for that same operation if the bill had gone to Medicare instead? Same operating room. Same surgeon. Same supplies. She didn't know. Nobody had ever told her there was another number, and the smaller one still keeps the hospital's lights on.

That gap is the whole story, and most school leaders have never been shown it exists.

I grew up around manual labor and a ranch, where you don't get to bill someone three times for the same job and call it market rate. That's the standard I expected when I got into insurance. It is not the standard most charter schools are getting on the single heaviest line in their health plan: hospital and facility costs.

Here is the mechanic, in plain English.

The plan most schools carry pays hospitals through a PPO network. The network negotiates a discount off the hospital's billed charges, and the plan pays the discounted amount. Sounds like a win. The trap is in the words "billed charges." A hospital's list price lives in a document called the chargemaster, and those prices are not tied to what care costs to deliver. They are set high on purpose. A 40 or 50 percent discount off a number that was inflated to begin with can still land far above what the service is worth. You can negotiate hard against a fake number and still overpay.

Reference-based pricing attacks that at the root. Instead of starting from the inflated list price and discounting down, the plan starts from a transparent benchmark and pays up from there. The benchmark is usually Medicare, which already calculates a defined rate hospitals accept every day. An RBP plan pays a set multiple of that rate, often somewhere around 120 to 200 percent of Medicare depending on the design. Commercial network pricing frequently runs several times Medicare, sometimes 300 percent or more. So you pay the hospital a fair, sustainable price and still pay far less than the network would have. You stop arguing about the discount and start defining the price.

I have to give you the tradeoff straight, because anyone selling this as free money is not being honest. Since an RBP plan pays off a benchmark instead of a signed network contract, a hospital can bill the member for the difference. That is called balance billing, and it is the real risk. A serious program manages it as a core feature, with member advocacy and a legal team that steps in on the employee's behalf when a bill shows up. Reputable administrators report most claims end with no balance bill to the member. Most is not never, and you should distrust anyone who promises never. This also only works on a self-funded chassis, where your plan, not an insurer, decides what it pays per claim.

So why was your school never shown this? Because RBP is more work. More work to design, more work to explain to staff, more work to support when a member calls about a bill. The easy-button renewal is to re-up the same network plan, pass along the increase, and move on. RBP never comes up, not because it wouldn't fit your school, but because it would ask something of the broker. That's the part I can't stomach, and it's most of why Charter Select reads your actual claims before recommending a single change.

I wrote the full breakdown up on our site. No jargon, no gate, free to read and free to forward to your board or business manager: **[LINK TO WHITE PAPER]**

If you do one thing with this, do this. Ask whoever handles your benefits one question.

On your largest hospital and facility claims last year, has anyone ever shown you what those services would have cost benchmarked against Medicare, next to what your plan actually paid? If no one has ever put those two numbers side by side, how would you know whether you have a cost problem, or just a broker who never looked?

If they can't answer that, you don't have a verdict yet. You have a question nobody on your account has bothered to ask on your behalf.

Aaron Schwen

---

*Posting notes: replace [LINK TO WHITE PAPER] with the live hub URL. Keep the sign-off personal. First comment should drop the link again (LinkedIn suppresses posts with outbound links in the body, so some people put the link in the first comment instead, test both).*

checklist.label: Company-page checklist / matrix
checklist.content_markdown:
# Company Page Spoke: Reference-Based Pricing Renewal Checklist

*Spoke type: Company storefront page (authoritative, technical, credibility-building). Goal: validate Charter Select to a prospect actively vetting us. Post from the Charter Select company page. Lead with the matrix, then the checklist.*

---

**Headline:** Your network discount is calculated off a price the hospital invented. Here is what it looks like to pay off a real benchmark instead.

Hospital and facility spend is the heaviest line in almost every charter school health plan. A PPO network discounts that spend off the hospital's chargemaster, a list price set high on purpose and not tied to the cost of care. Reference-based pricing (RBP) starts from a transparent Medicare benchmark and pays up from there. Here is how the two pricing models compare, and the checklist we run before recommending any move.

## Network pricing vs. reference-based pricing

| Factor | PPO network plan | Reference-based pricing |
|---|---|---|
| **Starting point** | Hospital's inflated chargemaster list price | Transparent Medicare benchmark |
| **How the price is set** | A discount negotiated off the list price | A defined multiple of Medicare, often 120 to 200 percent |
| **Typical result** | Frequently several times Medicare, sometimes 300 percent or more | A fair, sustainable price well below network levels |
| **Required chassis** | Fully insured or self-funded | Self-funded or level-funded only |
| **Main risk** | Overpaying quietly, year after year | Balance billing, managed by member advocacy and legal defense |
| **Reported savings** | Trimming the edges leaves the heaviest line untouched | Industry estimates commonly cite 20 to 30 percent of total plan spend |

You cannot run RBP on a fully insured policy. The insurer sets the price and keeps the upside, so there is no lever to pull. RBP requires a self-funded chassis where the plan, meaning your school, defines its allowable amount per claim.

## The renewal checklist

- [ ] Confirm the plan is self-funded or level-funded; RBP cannot bolt onto a fully insured policy.
- [ ] Pull your largest hospital and facility claims from last year and benchmark each against the Medicare rate.
- [ ] Compare what the plan actually paid to what the same service costs at a defined multiple of Medicare.
- [ ] Confirm the proposed RBP multiple in writing (commonly 120 to 200 percent of Medicare by service).
- [ ] Verify the balance-billing defense: member advocacy plus a patient-side legal team named in the contract, not promised verbally.
- [ ] Confirm the employee communication plan before launch, including who staff call the moment a balance bill arrives.
- [ ] Treat any single savings percentage as a marketing number until someone runs your real claims data.

## Why this is standard practice for us and rare elsewhere

A generalist broker re-ups the same network plan, passes along the increase, and moves on, because RBP is more work to design, explain, and support. We run your actual hospital claims against the Medicare benchmark, we put the balance-billing risk in front of you in plain language, and we confirm the advocacy and defense services are built in before recommending a single change. The heaviest line in your health plan is the one worth actively managing.

**Read the full breakdown:** [LINK TO WHITE PAPER]

---

*Posting notes: this is the credibility asset. Keep it clean and technical. The checklist also works as a downloadable PDF lead-capture on the website (top/bottom of the hub page). Replace [LINK TO WHITE PAPER] with the live URL.*

topics.title: The Health Plan That Eventually Owns Its Own Insurance Company
topics.subtitle: A plain-English look at group medical stop-loss captives: the funding endgame for a charter school health plan, the path that gets you there safely, the real players who built the model, and the honest risk a captive can absolutely go wrong.

hub.content_markdown:
# The Health Plan That Eventually Owns Its Own Insurance Company

*A plain-English look at group medical stop-loss captives: the funding endgame for a charter school health plan, the path that gets you there safely, the real players who built the model, and the honest risk a captive can absolutely go wrong.*

---

Two charter schools, same size, same town, both self-funded.

One hands its stop-loss premium to a carrier every year and watches it disappear into someone else's profit. The other pools that same layer of risk with a few dozen employers like it. In a healthy year, a check comes back with the school's name on it, plus a share of the interest the money earned while it sat in reserve.

Same plan underneath. Same employees, same claims, same coverage. The only difference is who owns the insurance company sitting on top of it.

---

## Where this fits, and where it doesn't

You may have read the other two papers in this set. One walks the funding spectrum from fully insured to level-funded to captives. The other reads the stop-loss contract line by line. This paper goes deeper on the destination itself. A group medical stop-loss captive is, for the right school, the endgame. Let me show you what it actually is, who built the model worth joining, how you get there without getting hurt, and where it breaks if you skip the homework.

## What a group stop-loss captive actually is

Start with what sits underneath, because a captive does not replace your plan. It sits on top of it.

Your school self-funds. You pay your employees' medical claims out of your own claims account instead of buying a fixed premium and handing the risk to a carrier. To cap the downside, you buy stop-loss insurance, which reimburses you when one person's claims or the whole group's claims blow past a set point. That self-funded structure is what most schools should reach first.

A group captive changes who carries the stop-loss layer. Instead of buying it from a carrier that keeps every dollar of profit, a group of similar employers form a member-owned insurance entity and pool a slice of their stop-loss risk inside it. Picture it in layers. Each school funds its routine claims at the bottom, up to a set specific deductible. Above that sits a shared middle layer inside the captive, where mid-sized claims get pooled across all the members, so one school's rough year is cushioned by the others. Above that, the captive buys reinsurance to catch the truly catastrophic claims no pool wants to carry alone.

Here is the part fully insured never gives you. If the pooled layer collects more than it pays out, that underwriting surplus comes back to the members, generally pro rata based on what each put in, plus the investment income the money earned while it sat in reserve. You stop being a premium payer and start being an owner who shares in the result and the data. Roundstone, which pioneered the group medical captive model in 2005, describes the captive layer as a shock absorber that smooths the volatility of mid-sized claims while reinsurance handles the catastrophic end. That is an accurate way to hold it in your head.

## The founder's path: level-funded first, captive as the goal

I do not put a school into a captive on day one. I think that is reckless, and I think most brokers who do it are chasing a story instead of protecting a client.

The path I believe in starts with level-funded. You step off fully insured into a plan where a healthy year pays you back, you get real monthly claims data for the first time, and you prove out your own claims story over a year or two with the budget predictability of a set monthly payment. Stop-loss is still doing the heavy lifting, so your downside stays capped close to what you already budgeted while you get comfortable being a self-funded employer.

Then, when the data says you are a good risk and your leadership is comfortable, you graduate into a captive. That is the endgame for the right school: a clean claims history, the cash position to ride a swing, and an appetite for the ownership upside and leverage of a member pool. Going level-funded first is not a detour. It is the runway that lets you walk in with your eyes open and your numbers proven. Every serious captive operator says the same thing in their own materials: the employers who do best join with detailed claims data, engaged leadership, and a genuine multi-year commitment, not the ones who jump in cold.

## The players who built this, and what each one stands for

The operators in this space are not all the same kind of thing. Some are captives you join. Some are consultants who advise the structure. Here are the ones worth naming and the principle each one champions.

**ParetoHealth** applies the member-owned group self-insurance idea to healthcare and runs it at scale. Its structure, which it calls a Risk Shield, stacks the same three layers: each employer carries its claims up to a specific deductible, the Pareto captive layer absorbs the mid-sized claims across the membership, and stop-loss reinsurance catches the catastrophic end. Pareto pools employers in roughly the fifty to a thousand employee band, which is exactly where most charter schools sit, and that scale is the point. A bigger pool means real negotiating leverage with carriers and vendors, more stability when one member has a brutal year, and group purchasing power no single school could touch alone. Pareto leans hard into education and data transparency too, with members getting claims data and pharmacy rebate visibility so they see where the dollars go. In my experience, Pareto is the best of the group. That is my opinion as someone who has placed this coverage, not a guarantee, and you should pressure-test it against your own situation.

**Captive Resources** is the consultant model, not a captive you join directly. It advises and helps manage member-owned group captives, and the principle it has championed for decades is genuine member ownership. The members own the captive outright, each gets a seat and a vote on the board regardless of premium size, and member committees oversee the real levers: underwriting, investment, and risk control. Premiums are built off each member's own loss experience rather than a blended rate, and unused loss funds come back as dividends, along with the investment income earned on them. The whole philosophy ties dollars to behavior: control your losses, keep more of your own money.

**Roundstone** pioneered the group medical captive in 2005 and was the first to open the model to employers as small as twenty-five lives. Its banner is transparency: transparent claims data so you can find your own cost drivers, transparent pharmacy, and surplus returned to members as cash distributions when the pooled layer runs ahead of claims. Roundstone built the small-and-midsize on-ramp the rest of the field now competes on.

**Evergreen** works the alternative-funding and captive mid-market for the same fifty to five hundred employee band, building multi-year benefit strategies off deep claims analysis rather than one-year renewals. Its positioning is the long game: the plan as a project you actively manage, not a number you re-quote every fall.

The point of naming them is not to crown a winner for your school sight unseen. It is to show you the field is mature and the same principles run through all of it: member ownership, surplus and investment income flowing back to members, transparent data, and loss control as the thing that earns the return. Which one fits depends on your size, your state, and your appetite, and that is a conversation, not a brochure.

## Be honest: a captive can go wrong

Anybody who sells you a captive as guaranteed savings is lying to you. A captive moves real risk onto your school. In a genuinely tough claims year, your school can owe more than its best case, in the form of additional contribution to the pooled layer, before reinsurance takes over. The ceiling is defined in advance, which is the whole point of the structure, but it is higher than a flat fully insured premium in a bad year. If your staff hit a brutal run of claims and the program around you is sloppy, a captive can cost you more, not less.

So before I put a school anywhere near one, three guardrails are non-negotiable.

**A tight plan document.** Your plan document is the legal rulebook for what gets paid, who is eligible, and on what terms. In a self-funded captive, every dollar paid out is partly your dollar and partly the pool's. A loose or outdated plan document means claims get paid that should have been questioned, and that leakage comes straight out of your result and your fellow members' surplus. A document drafted and maintained on purpose is the difference between a plan that controls its own spend and one that pays whatever lands on the desk.

**An independent PBM.** Pharmacy is one of the fastest-rising costs in any health plan, and the pharmacy benefit manager controls a huge share of it. A PBM bundled in by the carrier can profit off spread pricing and retained rebates, which means it makes more when your plan spends more. An independent, pass-through PBM passes the actual drug cost through and charges a flat fee, so its incentives line up with yours. This is exactly why the better captives push pharmacy transparency and rebate visibility as a core feature. In a structure where you keep the savings, an independent PBM is one of the strongest levers you have.

**Real cost-control mechanisms.** A captive without active cost containment is a pool waiting to overspend. The whole member-owned premise is that controlling your losses is what earns the dividend back, so the controls are not optional. That means care management on high-cost claimants, specialty drug strategies, network and reference-based pricing decisions, and the freedom to carve out vendors and switch them without rebuilding the whole plan. These limit the damage when your staff hit a hard stretch, and the model is built to let you use them. The school that joins a captive and runs it passively gets the risk without the controls that justify taking it.

## Why your generalist never built the runway

Here is the broker behavior that keeps schools stuck. The generalist who runs benefits as a side dish does not build the path from level-funded to captive, because that path is work. It means analyzing claims data every month, proving out a story over years, drafting a tight plan document, carving out a real PBM, and managing the program instead of mailing a renewal.

So they keep you fully insured and pass along the increase, because re-quoting the same plan is the easy button. They will tell you a captive is too advanced for a school your size, when the operators who built this model open it to employers as small as twenty-five lives. The runway exists. They just never bothered to build it, and you were never told it was an option.

## Before you renew the same plan one more time

You do not have to commit to a captive to find out whether one is in your future. You need someone willing to start the runway and show you the numbers at each step.

So sit with this one.

Has anyone ever laid out a multi-year path for your school, level-funded first to prove your claims story, then a captive as the goal, and shown you in dollars what the ownership upside and the honest downside would look like at each stage? Or has every renewal just been the same fully insured plan with a bigger number on it, because building the path was more work than your broker wanted to do?

If the honest answer is the second one, you do not have a benefits problem yet. You have a destination nobody ever offered to drive you toward.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. We build the runway on purpose, level-funded first to prove your numbers, then a captive when and only when it fits, with a tight plan document, an independent PBM, and real cost controls in place before you ever take on the risk. We show you the downside in dollars, not just the upside, so you decide with the whole picture in front of you.*
