newsletter.content_markdown:
# LinkedIn Personal Newsletter: The Health Plan That Owns Its Own Insurance Company

*Spoke type: Personal profile newsletter (Aaron's voice). Goal: human story + compressed insight, driving to the full white paper. Post from Aaron's personal LinkedIn. ~750 words.*

---

**Headline:** Two charter schools, same size, same town. One mails its premium off forever. The other gets a check back with its own name on it.

---

I sat across from a school business manager last spring who had just opened her renewal. Same fully insured plan, bigger number, like clockwork. She asked me a question I hear all the time: "Is this just what it costs now?"

So I drew her two schools on a napkin.

Both self-funded. Both the same size, same town, same kind of staff. The first one hands its stop-loss premium to a carrier every year and watches it vanish into someone else's profit. The second one pools that same slice of risk with a few dozen employers like it. In a healthy year, a check comes back to the second school with its own name on it, plus a share of the interest the money earned while it sat in reserve.

Same plan underneath. Same employees, same claims, same coverage. The only thing different is who owns the insurance company sitting on top.

That second structure is a group medical stop-loss captive, and for the right school it is the endgame.

Here is the mechanic in plain English. A captive does not replace your plan. It sits on top of it. Your school self-funds, paying claims out of your own account instead of buying a fixed premium. To cap the downside, you buy stop-loss. A group captive changes who carries that stop-loss layer. Instead of renting it from a carrier that keeps every dollar of profit, a group of similar employers form a member-owned entity and pool a slice of the risk inside it. Each school funds its routine claims at the bottom. A shared middle layer cushions the mid-sized claims across all the members, so one school's rough year gets absorbed by the pool. Reinsurance catches the truly catastrophic claims at the top.

When that pooled layer collects more than it pays out, the surplus comes back to the members, plus the investment income earned along the way. You stop being a premium payer and start being an owner.

Now the part I am stubborn about. I do not put a school into a captive on day one. I think that is reckless, and I think most brokers who try it are chasing a story instead of protecting a client.

The path I believe in starts with level-funded. You step off fully insured into a plan where a healthy year pays you back, you get real monthly claims data for the first time, and you prove out your own claims story over a year or two with the budget predictability of a set monthly payment. Stop-loss is still doing the heavy lifting, so your downside stays capped close to what you already budgeted. Then, when the data says you are a good risk and your leadership is comfortable, you graduate into the captive. Going level-funded first is not a detour. It is the runway that lets you walk in with your eyes open and your numbers proven.

And let me be honest about the downside, because anybody who sells a captive as guaranteed savings is lying to you. A captive moves real risk onto your school. In a genuinely brutal claims year you can owe more than your best case before reinsurance kicks in. The ceiling is set in advance, but it is higher than a flat premium in a bad year. That is exactly why I will not put a school near one without three things locked in first: a tight plan document, an independent pass-through PBM, and real cost controls running on the high-cost claims. Skip those and a captive can cost you more, not less.

I grew up around manual labor and a ranch, where a job done halfway meant somebody got hurt or something broke. That is the standard I expected when I got into insurance. Building the runway from level-funded to a captive is work, and the generalist who runs benefits as a side dish never builds it. So they keep you fully insured and pass along the increase, then tell you a captive is too advanced for a school your size, when the operators who built this model open it to employers as small as twenty-five lives.

I wrote the full breakdown on our site. No jargon, no gate, free to read and forward to your board: **[LINK TO WHITE PAPER]**

If you do one thing with this, sit with one question.

Has anyone ever laid out a multi-year path for your school, level-funded first to prove your numbers, then a captive as the goal, and shown you in dollars what both the upside and the honest downside would look like at each stage? Or has every renewal just been the same plan with a bigger number, because building the path was more work than your broker wanted to do?

If the honest answer is the second one, you do not have a benefits problem yet. You have a destination nobody ever offered to drive you toward.

Aaron Schwen

---

checklist.label: Company-page checklist / matrix
checklist.content_markdown:
# Company Page Spoke: Stop-Loss Captive Readiness Matrix & Checklist

*Spoke type: Company storefront page (authoritative, technical, credibility-building). Goal: validate Charter Select to a prospect actively vetting us. Post from the Charter Select company page. Lead with the matrix, then the checklist.*

---

**Headline:** A group medical captive is the funding endgame for the right charter school. It is also real risk transferred onto your balance sheet. Here is how to tell where your school actually belongs, and what has to be true before you move.

A group stop-loss captive can return underwriting surplus and investment income to your school instead of a carrier's shareholders. It can also cost you more than a flat premium in a brutal claims year. The difference between those two outcomes is readiness, not luck. Below is the funding spectrum staged from lowest control to highest, the readiness guardrails that have to be in place, and the checklist we run before recommending any move up the ladder.

## The funding spectrum, staged

| Stage | Who carries the risk | Healthy-year upside | Bad-year exposure | Data you get | Best fit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fully insured** | Carrier keeps premium and profit | None. Premium is gone either way | Capped at premium, but you overpay in good years | Little to none | Schools not ready to manage a plan |
| **Level-funded** | You self-fund claims; stop-loss caps the downside | Healthy year pays you back a share | Capped close to your budgeted monthly | Real monthly claims data, first time | The runway. Prove your claims story here first |
| **Group captive** | You + member pool own the stop-loss layer | Underwriting surplus + investment income returned to members | Higher than a flat premium in a bad year, ceiling set in advance | Full claims + pharmacy transparency | Clean claims history, cash to ride a swing, multi-year commitment |

The order matters. We do not jump a school from fully insured straight into a captive. Level-funded is the runway that proves the numbers and builds the habit of managing a self-funded plan before ownership risk goes on the table.

## The three readiness guardrails (non-negotiable before a captive)

| Guardrail | Why it protects your result | What "ready" looks like |
|---|---|---|
| **A tight plan document** | In a self-funded captive, every dollar paid out is partly yours and partly the pool's. A loose document leaks claims that should have been questioned | Drafted and maintained on purpose, eligibility and terms current, not a carrier's stale boilerplate |
| **An independent pass-through PBM** | A bundled PBM profits off spread pricing and retained rebates, so it earns more when you spend more | Flat-fee, pass-through pricing, full rebate and cost visibility |
| **Real cost-control mechanisms** | Controlling losses is what earns the dividend back. A passive captive is a pool waiting to overspend | Care management on high-cost claimants, specialty drug strategy, reference-based pricing options, freedom to carve out and switch vendors |

## The readiness checklist

- [ ] Confirm the school is off fully insured and has run level-funded long enough to produce a credible claims story (generally one to two years).
- [ ] Pull and review monthly claims data; identify cost drivers, not just the bottom-line number.
- [ ] Confirm a cash position that can absorb a bad-year additional contribution before reinsurance attaches.
- [ ] Draft or refresh the plan document so eligibility and payment terms are deliberate and current.
- [ ] Carve out an independent, pass-through PBM with full rebate visibility.
- [ ] Stand up real cost controls: high-cost claimant management, specialty drug strategy, network/RBP decisions.
- [ ] Model the captive in dollars at both ends, the ownership upside in a healthy year and the honest additional-contribution exposure in a bad one.
- [ ] Confirm leadership understands and accepts a genuine multi-year commitment before binding.
- [ ] Match the operator (Pareto, Captive Resources, Roundstone, Evergreen and others) to the school's size, state, and appetite, not the other way around.

## Why this is standard practice for us

A generalist broker keeps schools fully insured because re-quoting the same plan is the easy button, and then calls a captive too advanced for a school your size, even though the operators who built this model open it to employers as small as twenty-five lives. We build the runway on purpose: level-funded first to prove the numbers, then a captive when and only when the guardrails are in place, with the downside modeled in dollars before anyone takes on the risk.

**Read the full breakdown:** [LINK TO WHITE PAPER]

---

*Posting notes: this is the credibility asset. Keep it clean and technical. The matrix and checklist also work as a downloadable PDF lead-capture on the website (top/bottom of the hub page). Replace [LINK TO WHITE PAPER] with the live URL.*

topics.title: A Half-Million-Dollar Claim, a Policy That "Covered" It, and a Bill Your School Pays Anyway
topics.subtitle: How the fine print in a stop-loss contract decides whether your safety net actually catches a catastrophic claim, and why the broker who only quoted you a premium never walked you through the part that matters.

hub.content_markdown:
# A Half-Million-Dollar Claim, a Policy That "Covered" It, and a Bill Your School Pays Anyway

*How the fine print in a stop-loss contract decides whether your safety net actually catches a catastrophic claim, and why the broker who only quoted you a premium never walked you through the part that matters.*

---

A teacher's spouse gets a cancer diagnosis. The treatment runs past half a million dollars over eighteen months. Your school self-funds its health plan, and you bought stop-loss insurance for exactly this reason.

The claim comes in. It gets denied at the stop-loss level. Not because the treatment wasn't covered by your health plan, it was, but because of where the dates landed and what your contract said about them.

Your school eats the difference. And the contract that did it was sitting in your file the whole time, signed, with terms nobody ever read to you out loud.

---

## What stop-loss is actually doing

When your school self-funds, you pay your employees' medical claims directly out of your own money instead of handing a fixed premium to an insurer and letting them carry the risk. Level-funding works the same way underneath, with the bills smoothed into steady monthly payments. Either way, the school is now on the hook for claims.

That's a smart move for a lot of schools, because in a healthy year the savings stay in your budget instead of becoming an insurer's profit. But it raises an obvious question. What happens when a single catastrophic claim, or a brutal claims year overall, blows past what you can absorb?

That's what stop-loss insurance is for. It's the policy that caps your downside so one sick employee or one bad year can't sink the plan. The protection sounds simple. The protection you actually get is defined entirely by the contract terms, and that's where schools get hurt.

## Two kinds of protection, and you need both

Stop-loss comes in two parts, and they guard against two different disasters.

Specific stop-loss is your per-person cap. You pick a specific deductible, say a fixed dollar amount per individual. The school pays each person's claims up to that number, and the stop-loss carrier reimburses you for anything above it on that one person. This is what catches the single huge claim, the cancer case, the premature baby in the NICU, the transplant.

Aggregate stop-loss is your whole-plan cap. The carrier estimates your expected claims for the year and sets an attachment point above that, commonly around 125 percent of expected. If your total claims for everybody combined blow past that point, aggregate kicks in. This is what catches death by a thousand cuts, a year where no single claim is catastrophic but the pile of them together runs way over budget.

Specific guards one person. Aggregate guards the whole pool. A school carrying only one of them is half-covered and usually doesn't know it.

## The dates that decide whether you're covered at all

Here is the term that quietly denies real claims, and the one a generalist almost never explains.

A medical claim has two dates that matter. The date it was incurred, meaning when the care actually happened, and the date it was paid, meaning when the claim got processed and money went out. Those can be months apart. A big hospital stay in November might not finish paying out until the following spring.

Your stop-loss contract has a basis that defines which claims it covers using those two dates. You'll see it written as two numbers, like 12/12, 12/15, 24/12, or 15/12. The first number is the window for when a claim can be incurred. The second is the window for when it can be paid.

- A 12/12 contract covers claims both incurred and paid inside the same twelve months. It's the tightest window, and it's common for a first plan year.
- A 12/15 contract covers claims incurred during the twelve-month year and paid within fifteen months, giving you three extra months of pay-out runway. This is run-out protection. It catches the claims that happened during your year but settle after it ends.
- A 24/12 or 15/12 contract is run-in. It covers claims paid during your year even if they were incurred before the contract started. This matters when you're switching into a new carrier and still have old claims settling.

Now picture a carrier switch with neither run-in nor run-out lined up. Your old policy was 12/12. It closes the day your year ends. A claim that was incurred in your old year but hasn't finished paying yet falls past the old contract's window. Your new carrier's contract only covers claims incurred on or after its start date, so it won't pick that claim up either. The claim is real, the care was covered by your health plan, and yet it lands in a seam between two stop-loss contracts where neither one pays.

The school covers it. That gap isn't bad luck. It's a contracting decision that should have been made on purpose, in advance, by someone whose job was to make it.

## Lasering, where the carrier moves your goalposts on one person

Say you have an employee already in treatment for something expensive, a known high-cost claimant the carrier can see coming. At renewal, the stop-loss carrier can single that person out and assign them a higher individual deductible than everyone else. That move is called a laser.

The mechanics are blunt. Everyone else on the plan might sit at one specific deductible, and the lasered individual gets set far higher. The school now absorbs much more of that one person's claims before stop-loss reimbursement starts. Your exposure on the exact member you most need protection for goes up, sometimes by a lot.

Lasering is a legitimate underwriting tool, not a scam. But it can wreck a school's budget if it shows up as a surprise at renewal, right when you have the least leverage to do anything about it. Which is why the contract terms around lasering are worth real money:

- A no-laser provision means the carrier agrees not to laser anyone for the coming term.
- A no-new-laser provision means they won't add new lasers at the next renewal, though existing ones can carry forward and they may laser again in later years.
- A rate cap puts a ceiling on how much your renewal premium can jump, often paired with a no-new-laser provision so the carrier can't simply reprice you into the same pain a laser would have caused.

These protections cost something. Carriers add a premium load to provide them. The point isn't that every school should buy every guarantee. The point is that whether you have them, and what they cost, is a decision your school should get to make with eyes open, not discover after a laser lands.

## The other lever: aggregating specific deductibles

One more structure worth knowing, because it's sold as savings and it shifts risk back to you. An aggregating specific deductible lowers your specific premium by adding a second layer of retention. After an individual passes the regular specific deductible, the school keeps paying those excess amounts until a separate pooled dollar amount is used up, and only then does the carrier reimburse as usual.

It can genuinely lower premium. It can also mean the school pays more out of pocket in a heavy claims year than a quick premium comparison would ever suggest. Whether it fits depends on your cash position and your appetite for that swing. It is not a free discount, and it should never be quoted as one.

## Why the generalist sells you the net with the holes in it

A broker who runs benefits as a side dish to everything else does the easy version of this job. They collect a few stop-loss quotes, hand you the lowest premium, and call it a placement. The premium is the one number they can compare on a spreadsheet, so it's the only number they sell on.

The contract basis, the run-in and run-out terms, the laser provisions, the aggregating specific, all of that is where the actual protection lives, and all of it takes time to read, explain, and negotiate. So it gets skipped. You find out the terms were wrong the day a big claim drops through the gap, and by then the renewal is signed and the leverage is gone.

That's not a knowledge problem on your end. You run a school. It's the job you were paying someone else to do, and they quietly did the half that fits on a quote sheet.

## Before your next stop-loss renewal

You don't need to become a stop-loss underwriter to protect your school here. You need to ask a few plain questions and watch whether the answers come back fast and clear or slow and vague.

So sit with this one for a second:

If a claim incurred this plan year doesn't finish paying until after you switch stop-loss carriers, who pays it, your old contract, your new one, or your school? And the harder question underneath it: has anyone on your account actually walked you through your contract basis and your laser terms, in plain language, or did you just get handed the lowest premium and a place to sign?

If you can't answer that with confidence, you don't have a claim problem yet. You have a contract nobody read to you. The reading takes an afternoon. What it costs to skip it doesn't show up until a half-million-dollar claim is already on your desk.

---

*Charter Select works exclusively with charter schools across the country, handling both property & liability and employee benefits under one roof. If you want someone to read your stop-loss contract terms line by line before your next renewal, and tell you in plain English where the gaps are, that's the work we do before a claim ever forces the question.*

checklist.content_markdown:
# Company Page Spoke: Stop-Loss Contract Terms and Lasering Renewal Checklist

*Spoke type: Company storefront page (authoritative, technical, credibility-building). Goal: validate Charter Select to a prospect actively vetting us. Post from the Charter Select company page. Lead with the matrix, then the checklist.*

---

**Headline:** Self-funded with stop-loss? The terms decide whether your safety net catches a catastrophic claim. The premium quote never tells you that.

When a charter school self-funds or level-funds, stop-loss insurance is the policy that caps the downside on a single catastrophic claim or a brutal claims year. The protection you actually get is defined entirely by the contract terms, not the premium. Here is how the terms that matter compare, and the checklist we run on every stop-loss renewal.

## The stop-loss terms that decide whether you are covered

| Term | What it does | Why it matters to a school |
|---|---|---|
| **Specific stop-loss** | Per-person cap; carrier reimburses claims above your specific deductible on any one individual | Catches the single catastrophic claim (cancer, NICU, transplant) |
| **Aggregate stop-loss** | Whole-plan cap, commonly set near 125% of expected claims | Catches a year where many mid-size claims together blow past budget |
| **Contract basis (e.g. 12/12, 12/15, 24/12)** | Defines which claims are covered by when they were incurred and when they were paid | A carrier switch with no run-in or run-out leaves claims in a seam neither contract pays |
| **Run-out (e.g. 12/15)** | Covers claims incurred in your year but paid after it ends | Protects the big claim that happens late in the year and settles months later |
| **Run-in (e.g. 24/12, 15/12)** | Covers claims paid in your year even if incurred before the contract started | Protects you when switching carriers with old claims still settling |
| **No-laser / no-new-laser provision** | Carrier agrees not to laser anyone, or not to add new lasers at next renewal | Stops a surprise deductible spike on your highest-cost member |
| **Rate cap** | Ceiling on how much renewal premium can jump | Blocks the carrier from repricing you into the same pain a laser would cause |
| **Aggregating specific deductible** | Lowers specific premium by adding a second pooled retention layer the school pays first | Sold as savings; can cost more out of pocket in a heavy claims year |

A laser is when the carrier singles out a known high-cost claimant at renewal and assigns them a higher individual deductible than everyone else. It is a legitimate underwriting tool. The damage comes when it lands as a surprise, after the leverage to negotiate is gone.

## The renewal checklist

- [ ] Confirm you carry both specific and aggregate stop-loss, not just one.
- [ ] Pull the contract basis on the current policy in writing (the two-number window, e.g. 12/12).
- [ ] On any carrier switch, confirm run-in or run-out coverage so no claim falls into a seam between contracts.
- [ ] Identify any known high-cost claimants the carrier can see coming before renewal terms arrive.
- [ ] Confirm whether the renewal carries lasers, and price no-laser, no-new-laser, and rate-cap options.
- [ ] Check for an aggregating specific deductible and model the worst-case out-of-pocket, not just the premium savings.
- [ ] Read the full contract before binding, not the quote summary.
- [ ] Document the confirmed basis and laser terms for the board record.

## Why this is standard practice for us and rare elsewhere

A generalist broker chasing a lower premium collects a few quotes, hands over the lowest number, and calls it a placement. The basis, the run-in and run-out terms, and the laser provisions take an afternoon to read, explain, and negotiate, so they get skipped. We read the full stop-loss contract on every renewal and walk you through the basis and laser terms in plain language, because the cost of getting this wrong does not show up until a half-million-dollar claim is already on the desk.

**Read the full breakdown:** [LINK TO WHITE PAPER]

---

hooks.label: Short-form hook posts
hooks.content_markdown:
# Short-Form Hooks: Stop-Loss Terms and Lasering

*Spoke type: short-form text posts. Each is standalone, pattern-interrupt, drives to the hub. Numbered to match the distribution-schedule.csv. Replace [LINK] with the live white paper URL.*

---

**Hook 1**
A teacher's spouse got cancer. The treatment ran past $500,000.
The school had stop-loss insurance for exactly this.
The carrier still denied it and sent the bill back to the school.
One contract term did that: [LINK]

---

**Hook 2**
Your stop-loss contract has two numbers on it, like 12/12 or 12/15.
Most school leaders have never had them explained.
Those two numbers decide whether a catastrophic claim gets paid or bounced back to you.
Here is what they mean: [LINK]

---

**Hook 3**
You switched stop-loss carriers and saved on premium.
A claim incurred last year hasn't finished paying yet.
Your old contract closed. Your new one won't touch it.
Guess who pays. [LINK]

---

**Hook 4**
At renewal, a stop-loss carrier can single out your sickest employee.
They assign that one person a much higher deductible than everyone else.
It's called a laser, and it lands when you have the least leverage to fight it.
How to see it coming: [LINK]

---

**Hook 5**
Specific stop-loss catches the one huge claim.
Aggregate catches the year where small claims pile up past budget.
A school carrying only one of them is half-covered and usually doesn't know it.
Check which one you're missing: [LINK]

---

**Hook 6**
"Aggregating specific deductible" gets sold to schools as savings.
It lowers your premium by quietly handing you a second layer of risk to pay first.
In a heavy claims year it can cost more than the discount ever saved.
Read before you sign: [LINK]

---

**Hook 7**
One question for whoever handles your school's benefits:
If a claim incurred this year pays out after you switch stop-loss carriers, who covers it, your old contract, your new one, or your school?
If the answer is a shrug, read this: [LINK]

---

*Posting notes: these are channel-portable (LinkedIn, X, Facebook). On LinkedIn, consider putting [LINK] in the first comment rather than the post body to avoid reach suppression. Each hook is built to stand alone, so order can flex.*
